Deliverables

Every iGEM Team is responsible for completing the deliverables listed below. Some of these items are used to evaluate your team, in addition to the other medal and award criteria. Failure to complete these deliverables by the relevant deadlines may impact your team’s chance at winning medals and prizes.

Note: "Freeze" deadlines are hard deadlines that will not be extended. No exceptions!

Deliverables Dashboard

View the status of your deliverables on the deliverables dashboard feature on your team page to:

See when new deliverables are ready to edit.
View the submission status of each deliverable.
Manually mark certain deliverables as completed.
Toggle to view your deliverables as a list or as a timeline.

Access your team page via the team list.

Timeline view of the deliverables dashboard.
Village & Project Information

Due: June 24, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Each team must select a Village in which to participate. This is required for all teams.

Safety Forms

Preliminary Deadline: July 1, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Final Deadline: October 7, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

All teams are expected to follow a high standard of safe and responsible biological engineering, and to demonstrate this to iGEM by completing Safety Forms. Failure to complete Safety Forms will result in your team's disqualification from the competition.

Project Promotion Video

Due: August 12, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Produce a 2-minute introduction (Project Promotion) video about your project.

Jamboree Talk

Proposals due: September 16, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Teams can apply for stage time to present their work to the public during the Grand Jamboree. In-person teams only.

Expo Booth

Safety Proposals due: September 16, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Teams will be assigned a Expo Booth where they can talk about their project with other Jamboree attendees. In-person teams only.

Wiki

Freeze: October 21, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Wikis are websites where teams document their projects for the judges and the public to view. This is required for all teams.

Software

Freeze: October 21, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Submit your software to iGEM’s GitLab repository. This is required for all teams in the Software & AI Village, teams competing for the Best Software Tool prize, or any teams creating software tools.

Project Attributions Form

Freeze: October 21, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

You must clearly state the work that was done by the students on your team, and note any work that was done by people outside of your team. Missing or erroneous information may affect your team’s eligibility for awards and/or personal certificates.

Registry Contributions

Freeze: October 21, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Submit and document your Parts in the iGEM Registry of Standard Biological Parts. This is required for all teams designing or characterizing DNA Parts.

Judging Form

Freeze: October 21, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

To become eligible for medals and prizes, you must complete and submit your Team's Judging Form by this deadline. Failure to complete and submit the Judging Form prevents judges from evaluating your team for medals and/or prizes.

Presentation Video

Due: October 28, 2026 15:00 (UTC)

Produce a 15-minute Presentation Video for the judges and the public to view. This is required for all teams.

Judging Session

During 13th-16th November

Your Judging Session is when you’ll interact with your judges during the Grand Jamboree. This is required for all teams.

Judging in iGEM
PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

Judging in iGEM is an essential component of the iGEM Competition. A panel of judges is entrusted with evaluating each team's project. Based on these evaluations, teams earn medals, prizes and awards that acknowledge their hard work. Judging allows us to recognize and celebrate the efforts, accomplishments and excellence of iGEM teams.

How does Judging Work?

The information teams provide on the Judging Form is used to create ballots for Judges. Judges evaluate team projects and fill out these ballots based on established performance criteria. Different sections of the ballot are used to determine different medals and awards. Teams could win prizes in any or all of the following: Medals, Project Prizes (Grand Prize, Village Prizes), and Special Prizes.

Medals

Medals celebrate the accomplishments of all iGEM teams. Teams compete only with themselves for a medal by meeting the performance requirements at each level. The three levels of medals, from lowest to highest, are Bronze, Silver and Gold. You can learn about this on the Medals page.

Project Prizes

The Project Prizes include the Grand Prizes and Village Awards. These prizes celebrate the best overall teams. You can learn more about this on the Project Prizes page.

Special Prizes

Special Prizes are competitive and celebrate the best projects in specific areas. Learn more about this on the Special Prizes page.

Tips for Team Success
Learn About the Judging Criteria Early

Together with each of your teammates and instructors, you should learn about the judging criteria by which you will be evaluated by your judging panel. This is done by reading through all of the content provided for the Medals, Project Prizes and Special Prizes, as you begin your iGEM project. You can also review the Judging Handbook. Reviewing this material at the start of your project ensures that you can ask questions as early as possible. Learning about and understanding this material should be the responsibility of the entire team, not just a single person.

Document Your Work Correctly

It is your responsibility to convince the judges that your team has achieved the criteria for the medal and/or awards you are working towards. This is accomplished by documenting your work with care. All of your documentation must be clear and thorough. The documentation that has the biggest impact on judging includes your entire Team Wiki (including the Standard URL Pages), your Registry Part Pages (if applicable to your team), your Presentation Video and the Judging Form.

Wiki

Your Team Wiki is how you and your teammates will communicate your entire project to the world. For judges to specifically find your work, your wiki must include the standard URLs that correspond to your desired medals and awards. You can learn more about all of the details of your wiki on the Team Wiki page.

Parts

If your team worked with parts this year, you must clearly document your work on each part's Main Page on the Registry, so the judges can evaluate your work. You can learn more about Part Pages on the relevant deliverable page.

Team Presentation Video

The purpose of the presentation video is to communicate your project to others in an informative and engaging manner. You can learn more about this on the Presentation Video page.

Judging Form

The Judging Form is the primary way you will communicate your team's achievements to the judges. You will use this form to select desired prizes and provide the standard URLs for judges to find your work. You can learn more about this on the Judging Form page.

Standard URLs

We have created Standard URL Pages in your wiki template for the medal criteria and some of the Special Prizes. If your team wants to be evaluated for a medal and/or a Special Prize, you must document your work related to the medal criteria and/or Special Prize on these Standard URL Pages. These URLs are listed with each medal or prize requirement, and collectively on the Team Wiki page.

Avoid These Common Mistakes
Waiting Until the Last Minute

Do not wait until the last minute when it comes to key judging deadlines like the Wiki Freeze, Judging Form Freeze and Registry Part Pages Freeze (if applicable). Your team should work on these items throughout the iGEM Competition and not just in the day(s) leading up to the deadline. The Freezes are very important deadlines for judging and no extensions will be granted. (No exceptions!)

Waiting until the deadline is close to begin editing Wiki Pages, Part Pages, and the Judging Form, often leads to mistakes with no time to correct them, such as the following:

Incomplete, incorrect, or missing information on your Wiki pages, Judging Form and/or Registry Pages.
HTML or CSS errors that make the Wiki pages unreadable.

Consider using the day of the deadline to review your documentation and Judging Form, and to only make small changes, such as correcting spelling mistakes.

Failure to Review the Judging Form

Make sure you set aside enough time to fully review the Judging Form prior to the Judging Form Freeze. Teams should review the completed Judging Form together and click through all of the Standard URLs (and Part Pages, if you worked with parts) on the Judging Form to make sure your team has documented your work on the correct pages. This should be a team activity since the Judging Form reflects the work you’ve done as a team. This should not be the responsibility of a single person.

During this review, you may discover that you made a mistake on your Team Wiki and therefore need to edit a Standard URL page to fix it. This will take time, so please make sure you review your Judging Form three (3) days or more before the freeze deadlines so you have enough time to fix mistakes.

Beyond the Judge’s Ballot: Other Awards

iGEM Judges do not determine every prize. The iGEM Competition offers a few prizes that are awarded by the wider iGEM community. These prizes celebrate teams and their work in ways that a traditional judging panel would not be able to.

Community-Awarded Prizes
iGEMers' Prize

The iGEMers' Prize is voted on by each year’s iGEM teams to celebrate their favorite teams. Each iGEM team receives a ballot to select a team (other than their own!) that they believe best represents iGEM’s values. So, the choice is yours!

Best Project Promotion Video Prize

The Project Promotion Video summarizes a team’s project by introducing the problem the team is addressing, their solution and their project’s impact. The best Project Promotion Video Prize rewards creative use of video, quality of production and clear communication of the team’s project objectives. As with the iGEMers' Prize, the Best Project Promotion Video Prize is decided by the most votes from other iGEM Teams.

Chairman's Award

This award is given to the team that best embodies the values of iGEM. When and to which team the prize is awarded is at the full discretion of the President of iGEM. As an example, in 2014, the Chairman’s Award celebrated Team Sumbawagen. This team captured the community's respect and admiration due to their sense of integrity, honesty, effort, celebratory spirit and the dedication they showed throughout the Competition. You can see the whole story of Team Sumbawagen 2014 ↗ on the iGEM Video Universe.

High School Competition

PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

What's New for High School Competition in 2025?

We're excited to announce some major updates for the iGEM High School Competition in 2025! High school teams should consider these changes when designing their projects.

1. Projects should be based on White List

Starting in 2025, high school teams must ensure that their projects use only the organisms and components specified in the current iGEM White List. Additionally, all team activities must strictly align with the items included in the White List.

2. High School Teams Select & Join Villages

High school teams will choose their own Village and will display their projects alongside collegiate teams in the shared Village spaces during the Grand Jamboree, creating collaborative communities between the different competitions and various education levels.

3. High School PI Network

The High School PI network is open to any adult who is interested in building a supportive community focused on the High School iGEM experience. Planned activities will include; webinars, Q&A sessions with members of iGEM HQ, and the opportunity for PIs to share their experiences and resources with each other.

To describe why High School iGEM is so valuable for both students and their schools, several high school PIs wrote up a document explaining the import and impact of High School iGEM.

Download it here
Join the HS PI Network
Sign up for the HS PI Webinars

What do the Collegiate and High School Competitions have in common?
1. Deliverables

Teams must complete specific deliverables to qualify for the competition. Detailed descriptions of these deliverables can be found in the

Deliverables section on the website.

2. Judging

The competition judging process will remain largely the same, For the latest updates, visit the Judging section of the website for updates.

3. Jamboree Attendance

The Jamboree is both a celebration of your work and the largest gathering of the synthetic biology community. All teams are encouraged to attend the event from October 28-31, 2025, at the Paris Convention Centre, Paris, France.

4. Distribution Kits

Teams that register will receive a welcome package containing the Parts Distribution Kit, iGEM swag, and information from various partners and sponsors of iGEM.

5. Mentorship Program

The Mentorship Program is a great fit for NEW teams who would like to better understand the iGEM Competition through an experienced iGEM Mentor. The mentorship program covers the basics from competition deliverables to important deadlines, while also adding the mentor's own tips and professional experience to the mix.

6. iGEM Ambassadors

iGEM Ambassadors are individuals with firsthand experience in the competition. They represent regions worldwide and work on community-focused projects to reinforce and expand synthetic biology initiatives. Some ambassadors have high school competition experience and can serve as great resources.

title: Overview | iGEM Competition
content:
About

Registration
Registration Overview
iGEM 2026 Competition
Overview
Competition Types
To register, you’ll first choose which competition your team will enter. iGEM organizes two competition types, depending on what kind of students make up the team.
Collegiate Competition
For teams made up of collegiate-level students enrolled at universities, colleges, or similar post-secondary institutions.
High School Competition
For teams made up of high school-level students enrolled at high schools or other secondary education institutions.
Deadlines
Teams can register during the Early, Regular, or Late registration periods. These periods determine the Program Fee rate and the payment deadline for that rate. The Program Fee payment is required for a team to be accepted, along with other registration requirements listed below. We recommend starting registration early, even if you are planning to complete the payment in a later registration period.
More about Fees & Deadlines ↗
February 12
15:00 UTC
Early Registration Deadline
April 2
15:00 UTC
Regular Registration Deadline
June 4
15:00 UTC
Late Registration Deadline
Starting an Application
Team registration begins by creating a new team at teams.igem.org.
The registration must be done by the Primary PI. Students are not allowed to do the application on behalf of the PI.
Create a New Team
Registration Steps & Requirements
#1
Primary PI

The Primary Principal Investigator (PI) plays a key role in overseeing and supporting the team throughout the season. PIs provide guidance and oversight, including help with fundraising, lab access, and navigating institutional policies and administrative requirements.

The Primary PI is also the team’s official point of contact with iGEM and accepts responsibility for the team within their institution. iGEM may contact the PI regarding registration, safety, judging, or other important matters.

Eligibility to be a PI

Eligibility to serve as a Primary PI depends on your Organizer Type (higher education, secondary education, community lab, or commercial team).

Higher Education Teams: The Primary PI must be a senior representative of the institution, such as a professor, department head, or equivalent. Additional PIs can be post-doctoral researchers or higher.
Secondary Education Teams: The Primary PI must be a principal, teacher, or academic coach employed by the school where team members are enrolled. If students are from multiple schools the Primary PI must be a principal, teacher, or academic coach employed at of one of the schools.
Community Lab Teams: The Primary PI must be the director of the community lab.
Commercial Teams: The Primary PI must be a company employee authorized to make binding commitments.
Responsibilities of the PI

The Primary PI supports the team and serve as iGEM’s main point of contact throughout the season.

Team Registration: Completing and managing the team registration on the iGEM platform.
Communication with iGEM HQ: Serving as the primary contact for official communications.
Team Roster: Managing the team roster and ensuring all members are correctly registered.
Safety Certification: Certifying the team's adherence to safety protocols.
Team Support: Providing guidance, resources, and support throughout the iGEM season.
Payment Responsibilities: Creating orders and managing the payment of team fees.

While not required, having a Secondary PI is strongly recommended. A Secondary PI serves as an additional point of contact and holds the same level of authority as the Primary PI.

PI Agreement

Successful iGEM teams are supported by committed PIs. To codify these roles and responsibilities across the iGEM community, PIs have to affirm their commitment by agreeing to a standard set of terms during the Team Application.

The PI agreement outlines their roles and responsibilities, enabling participation in the iGEM program.

If a team changes their PI after the registration, the new PI also agrees that these conditions apply.

Read the PI Agreement ↗

#2
Institution/s

Team institutions are the official organizations that team members are affiliated with, such as a university, high school, community lab, company, or another recognized entity.

Why Institutions Matter

Institutions form the foundation of every iGEM team. Being officially affiliated with an institution formalizes a team’s participation and makes it possible for the team to exist, operate, and be represented within the competition.

During registration, you will be asked to list all institutions your team is affiliated with. This information also helps iGEM understand where teams are based and how the global iGEM community is represented each year.

Through the iGEM Competition, iGEM collaborates with institutions worldwide to advance education, research, and collaboration in synthetic biology.

Institution Requirements
Every team must be formally affiliated with at least one institution.
Teams may have more than one institution. All institutions represented on the team roster should be listed.
The Primary PI must have an active role within at least one of the institutions listed.
Team PI can add or edit institutions after the registration is submitted, up until the team roster freeze deadline.
Information Collected

For each institution in your team, the PI will be asked to provide:

Official Name, in English
Official Name, in Local Language
Official Abbreviation
Website Link
Department / Unit
City, State/Region, Country
#3
Location
Location (City & Country)

Each year, iGEM brings together teams from hundreds of cities and institutions around the world. In 2025, over 400 teams from more than 40 countries took part in iGEM, with more than 10,000 participants. Location information helps to understand, analyze trends, and showcase the global reach of the iGEM community - an essential part of the iGEM experience.

This information is collected for informational and analytical purposes only.
View the Team List ↗
About Team Location
There are no requirements or competition relevance tied to the city or country you select. The location does not affect eligibility, judging, or competition outcomes in any way.
If your team is associated with multiple cities or countries, please select only one primary location. For example, this may be the location of PI’s Institution.
The term "country" in normal commercial use may refer to a country, region, province, or other economic area and may be used to list teams publicly (e.g., on the team list) and for statistical analysis.
Based on your location, your team will be automatically allocated one of the iGEM regions: Asia & Oceania, Africa, Europe, Latin America, or North America. This too, has no effect on your competition outcome, and is just informational.
Collegiate Teams
High School Teams
#4
Organizer Type

Organizer type (previously known as “Team Kind”) describes how your team is organized and hosted. It determines which requirements and documentation apply to your team’s registration. Choosing the correct organizer type ensures your team is eligible to compete and registers accurately.

Why organizer types exist

iGEM teams come from many different settings, reflecting the diversity of how synthetic biology is taught, learned, and built around the world.

iGEM originally began as a university-level student competition before expanding to support teams from a wider range of institutional and organizational environments.

We want to allow teams from different circumstances to be able to take part in iGEM. To support this, iGEM establishes clear guidelines and requirements to ensure all teams participate under fair and transparent conditions.

About Organizer Types
Organizer type does not affect judging criteria or competition outcomes. All teams within the same competition (Collegiate or High School) compete together in the same competition, regardless of organizer type.
Some organizer types, such as Community Lab and Commercial, require additional documentation and forms to be eligible.
Registering your team with an incorrect organizer type will make your team ineligible for the competition.
Organizer Type Options
Higher Education
Teams affiliated with and organized by higher education or collegiate institutions, including universities, colleges, and other post-secondary education establishments.
Collegiate Competition Only
Secondary Education
Teams affiliated with and organized by high schools.
High School Competition Only
Community Lab
Teams affiliated with and organized by independent community labs.
More info about additional requirements ↗
Commercial*
Teams organized by companies, including companies contracting to run teams for colleges, universities, or other organizations, as well as companies recruiting individual members.
More info about additional requirements ↗
*All teams run by companies must register as commercial teams. Commercial teams registering as other organizer types are not eligible to compete. If you are a commercial team, please review the Commercial Teams page before starting your application.
#5
Team Name

Your team name is your team’s official identity within iGEM. It will be used throughout the season and preserved as part of your team’s record. Team names at iGEM do not indicate projects. Instead, they typically reflect a team’s geographic location or institutional affiliation. Your team name should identify where your team comes from, and not what your project is about.

Requirements
Must be based only on your:
city
school or institution name(s)
state, country, and/or region
Must be between 3 and 20 characters
Must use the Latin alphabet (A–Z, uppercase or lowercase)
Must start with an uppercase (capital) letter
Allowed
Letters (A-Z, upperscase or lowercase)
Numbers (0-9), Hyphen (-), Space
Not Allowed
Team names must not include:
Generic or descriptive words such as:
team, crew, squad, winner, prize
bio, biology, science, biotech
iGEM, igem, synthetic biology, synbio
Project names, mascot, project themes, sponsor names
Year references (e.g. 26 or 2026)
Any abbreviations that are not clearly related to institution/school, city, country, state, region.
Underscores (_) or other symbols, except hyphen (-) and space
Recommendations

Choosing a clear and consistent team name helps avoid confusion and makes it easier for teams to be recognized across the iGEM community.

If your team is the only team in your city or country, using the city or country name alone is a good choice.
If there are multiple teams from the same city or country, we recommend adding another clear identifier, such as an abbreviation of your institution or school.
If your team has participated in iGEM before, we recommend keeping the same team name for continuity and consistency, when possible and where it meets current naming guidelines.
Team names are used and read internationally. We recommend avoiding words that may be unclear, ambiguous, or have unintended meanings in English, even if they are common words in another language.
Please also avoid using transliterated or non-English equivalents of common words (such as connectors like “and,” “of,” or “in”).
Team names are reviewed on a first-come, first-served basis. We ask teams to be mindful of other teams when choosing a name, particularly in cities or regions with multiple teams. If a new team later forms in your city, country, or institution, it may become necessary to adjust or further distinguish your team name to avoid confusion. iGEM does not determine ownership of team names beyond the order in which they are submitted and approved.
Approval Process
All team names require approval, including names that may have been used in previous years.
In most cases, team names meet the guidelines and are approved quickly. In a small number of cases, iGEM may contact the PI if clarification or adjustments are needed.
The PI may edit/propose a new team name up until the team registration is fully accepted (completed all requirements, including team name approval).
Once your team is marked as Accepted, the team name becomes final and can no longer be changed.
#6
Payment Order

Registration fees are completed in three progressive and connected stages, guiding teams from registration and official start (Stage I); to finalization, evaluation and award eligibility (Stage II); and to the global showcase in person at the Jamboree (Stage III). Each stage represents a milestone in your team’s journey and ensures that all participants are fully supported, evaluated, and recognized for their work.

To complete registration, you must create a payment order for the Program Fee - Stage I (Registration) and complete the payment in full before the applicable deadline.

You can create your payment order during the registration process or later via payments.igem.org.
Only Team PIs (Primary or Secondary) can create or manage payment orders for a team.
More about Team Fees ↗
Create a New Payment Order
#7
Additional Requirements

After you submit your registration, you’ll get access to your Team Dashboard, where you can track the status of your team’s registration and any required submissions. Depending on your competition type and organizer type, your team may also need to complete additional requirements.

Shipping Address

The shipping address is used to send team materials during the competition season, including the iGEM DNA Distribution Kit - a kit of standard biological parts showcasing the collective work of the global iGEM community.

The shipping address is required to complete registration.
The shipping address must not be a personal home address.
The kit will be shipped after your team has been fully accepted.
You may edit the shipping address until the shipment processing begins.
More about the Distribution Kit ↗
Consent Forms

In addition to the general team registration requirements, each team must submit a set of consent forms. This is done directly through teams.igem.org. There are two types of consent forms:

For all teams:

Participant Consent Forms: Every participant must submit an individual consent form to join the Team Roster. Participants under age 18 (on March 31, 2026) cannot sign for themselves - a parent or legal guardian must sign on their behalf.

For High School Competition only (except Commercial Type Teams):

Principal Acknowledgment Agreements: At least one Principal Acknowledgment Agreement is required to complete registration.
Additional forms may are required for each school represented on the Team Roster.
More about Consent Forms ↗
Team Roster

The Team Roster is the official record of everyone who is involved with the iGEM Team, including professors, teachers, instructors, advisors and student team members.

Completing the roster is not required to submit your registration, but we recommend starting early.
Only Team PIs can manage Team Rosters.
Team PIs can access the roster immediately after submitting registration and can invite or accept team members.
Only individuals listed on the roster by the Roster Freeze deadline will receive participation certificates.
The final deadline to add or remove members is September 16. No changes are allowed after this date.
More about the Team Roster ↗
Questions?
If you have any questions about team registration, eligibility, payments, or required documents, contact iGEM HQ at hq@igem.org ↗.

How to Start an iGEM Team

Starting a team in iGEM can seem daunting at first. Here are some guidelines to help you get started.

If you have any questions, don’t hesitate to contact iGEM Headquarters for more information by emailing us at hq [AT] igem [DOT] org. We will be happy to hear from you!

1. Get Inspired
iGEM teams use synthetic biology to tackle local and global challenges. This team from University College London created a sustainable alternative to cement to decarbonize the construction industry. Team UCL at the 2022 Grand Jamboree, Paris.

Every year in the iGEM Competition, thousands of students around the world push the boundaries of synthetic biology by tackling both local and global challenges. With 100,000+ participants and 5,000+ projects built since 2004, the best way to get inspired is to look at past projects. You can:

Take a look at team wikis from previous years

Teams document their entire project on their team wiki, from their inspirations to project design, and results.

Watch team videos on the iGEM Video Universe

You can watch project promotion videos and presentation videos on the iGEM Video Universe ↗.

Explore stories on the iGEM Blog

These iGEM Blog stories highlight excellence in iGEM Projects around topics like biodiversity, the Sustainable Development Goals, climate change, human health, and more.

Search projects using the Phoenix Project search tool

Search and sort projects by different categories and awards; Preview project abstracts, and click through to the team wiki for more information about each project.

Note: The Phoenix Project search tool currently indexes projects up to and including 2022.

2. Recruit The Team
We recommend creating a team of around 8 - 15 people with different backgrounds who can enrich the team. Team Lethbridge HS at the 2022 Grand Jamboree, Paris.

To compete in iGEM, you will need to form a team. Here are some of the most common questions we get about team composition:

How many students do we need?

There is no upper limit on the number of students in an iGEM team. We recommend creating a team of around 8 - 15 people with different backgrounds who can enrich the team. We also recommend creating an ethnically- and gender-diverse team, with strong representation of women and non-binary members.

Must students have a specific academic background?

We recommend including team members from different academic backgrounds, ranging from biology and chemistry to mechanical engineering, industrial design, art, and philosophy. You may also want to recruit team members who can help build a website and produce videos. While there are no regulations about how to assign team duties, we recommend that all students have opportunities to try many different types of work during the project: modeling, human practices, bench work, web design, video production, etc.

Is there an age restriction?

All participants must be at least 13 years old by March 31 of their competition year. Student members are expected to be enrolled in a secondary or higher education institution to participate in iGEM. The exception are Community Lab team members, where the team is made up of local people working at the community lab. Also, be aware that the age of student team members affects the assignment of Undergraduate and Overgraduate sections in the Collegiate Competition.

Can more than one school be in one team?

Yes, two or more schools can join together to form a single team. The schools also do not need to be local to one another – we’ve had teams in the past with students from China and the United States on the same team! This allows a team to combine different backgrounds and talents to enrich the overall project. Just make sure to include all participants on the Team Roster.

How many Principal Investigators (“PIs”) do we need?

PIs are the official leads responsible for the team’s registration, safety oversight, payments, and compliance throughout the season. PIs approve roster members and act as the main point of contact with iGEM. You need at least one Primary PI to be part of your team, though we strongly recommend (but do not require) that you have more than one PI.

Other than students and PIs, who else should we include on an iGEM team?

You may want to include Instructors and/or Advisors who can help with technical training in lab protocols and support the team in understanding and meeting competition requirements (e.g., previous iGEMers, graduate students, post-docs, and/or lab technicians). If there are none around you, you may be interested in signing up for the Mentorship Program.

3. Find Space

Teams need a space in which to work during the season. You may want to:

Ask your instructors or teachers if they have lab space during the iGEM season (generally May – October, although some teams may start before then). Your team may want to start setting up dedicated lab space for your team to use this year and in future years.

See if your school or department has free lab space. Make sure that in addition to benches and equipment, you also have a space for meetings, relaxing, eating, etc.

Your team may want to start setting up dedicated lab space for your team to use this year and in future years. Photo by Trnava University on Unsplash.
4. Create A Budget And Begin Fundraising

There are several things you need to consider when creating a budget for your iGEM team, including Team Fees and Grand Jamboree tickets, as well as expenses for lab supplies, travel, and team expenses. Some expenses vary depending on region and country, and you may wish to consult with other iGEM teams from your area to get an idea of their budget.

Budgeting and Fundraising

Check out our fundraising guide for more details about what to consider when writing a budget and fundraising plan.

5. Read The Competition Requirements

Each team member, including students, advisors, instructors, and Primary and Secondary PIs, should take the time to read through the Competition requirements prior to starting work on your iGEM project.

Make sure that your team has reviewed all aspects of the rules and policies, deliverables, and deadlines, as some aspects may be very important to know before starting to plan your project.

We usually post Competition requirements by March of each year. Subscribe to the iGEM Newsletter to stay updated! ↗

As you embark on your iGEM journey, be prepared to work hard and have lots of fun! Though your journey will be unique to you and your teammates, know that you will not be alone. You’ll be in good company with fellow iGEMers and the thousands of iGEM alumni from all over the world who will help you along the way. Welcome to iGEM!

On this page

1. Get Inspired
2. Recruit The Team
3. Find Space
4. Create A Budget And Begin Fundraising
5. Read The Competition Requirements

About the Competition

The iGEM Competition is an annual, worldwide synthetic biology event aimed at undergraduate university students, as well as high school and graduate students. The iGEM Competition gives students the opportunity to push the boundaries of synthetic biology by tackling everyday issues facing the world. Multidisciplinary teams work together to design, build, test, and measure a system of their own design using interchangeable biological parts and standard molecular biology techniques.

iGEM teams work inside and outside the lab, creating sophisticated projects that strive to create a positive contribution to their communities and the world. iGEMers are building a better world by solving problems with the help of synthetic biology. This year, we expect over 11,000 people to dedicate months of their time and energy to their iGEM projects and then come together to present their work and compete at the Grand Jamboree.

Understanding the iGEM Competition

Together with each of your teammates, instructors, and PIs, you should go through the pages listed below. Reviewing this material at the start of your project ensures that you can ask questions as early as possible.

Learning about and understanding this material should be the responsibility of the entire team and not a single person.

Rules and Policies

Understanding and following all of the rules and policies is a requirement for every team who participates in iGEM. Read the Rules and Policies page for more information.

Starting a Team

Starting a team in iGEM can seem daunting at first, even if you are a returning member. Visit the Starting a Team page for more information.

Navigating iGEM

Starting an iGEM team and getting a project started can be a challenge even for returning teams. Visit the Navigating iGEM page for more information.

Fundraising

Now is the time to share the joy and excitement of your iGEM project with those who can help support and fund your team. Visit the Budgeting and Fundraising page for more information.

Collaborations

To help facilitate collaborations, we have made this page to help you share collaborations with your fellow iGEM teams. Visit the Collaborations page for more information.

Team Composition

Many disciplines come together in iGEM. The competition might seem exclusive to biology or engineering disciplines, but teams will often have students from very diverse fields to complement the project. A few examples are: computer science, art and design, physics, marketing, law, mathematics, and social sciences.

Teams are also required to have an academic instructor in order to participate. This requirement helps ensure that teams have enough mentorship support throughout the competition. They are also encouraged to look for more advisors and mentors that can help the team succeed with their project. This often includes graduate students and other professors from their university who have experience with one (or more) aspect of their project.

We also recommend creating an ethnically- and gender-diverse team, with strong representation of women and non-binary members. See the Diversity & Inclusion Committee's recommendations for creating a well-rounded and diverse team.

Safety

iGEM expects all teams to demonstrate to iGEM HQ, the wider community, and to anyone interested how you are working safely and securely. Teams do this by thinking carefully about and managing any risks to yourselves, your colleagues, community, or the environment. You need to continue to think about risks throughout your project and revisit these issues as your plans change. Visit the Responsibility site for more information.

Human Practices

Using synthetic biology to address real-world problems requires thoughtful engagement with the world. In iGEM, we call this engagement Human Practices. We ask every team to think deeply and creatively about whether their project is responsible and good for the world. Human Practices is not any specific activity or set of activities. While the most successful iGEM teams will engage with Human Practices issues throughout their project lifecycle, this engagement can take many shapes, reflecting the diverse contexts and intentions of iGEM projects. Visit the Responsibility site for more information.

Engineering

The engineering of a biological system is the central goal of any iGEM project. But engineering a biological system is a complex, challenging, and often slow process. In iGEM, teams follow the Design-Build-Test-Learn cycle in their work. Design principles are used to specify a biological system with an intended function, and models are used to help make a design according to these principles. Building your design is the next stage of a well-engineered project. Design testing by good measurement is essential to ensure that work done by iGEM teams can be used by future teams and the community as a whole. Now that you’ve finished running your Tests, it’s time to analyze your data and learn from your work. Visit the Technology site for more information.

The iGEM Registry

iGEM runs the Registry of Standard Biological Parts, which houses a growing collection of genetic parts that can be mixed and matched to build synthetic biology devices and systems. During the competition, iGEM teams have access to parts, samples, data, and tools to work on their synthetic biology projects. One of the expectations for participating iGEM teams is that they will submit any new biological parts they created to the Registry in order to help the Registry grow. Teams are also encouraged through the medal system to characterize their new parts along with existing parts from the Registry and submit that data to the Registry in order to help future teams use those parts.

Celebrating Team Achievements

At the Jamboree, iGEM teams compete for various awards, including Medals and Special Prizes. Each team will have their project judged by a panel of experts in the field of synthetic biology, who help us celebrate the achievements of the iGEM teams by awarding them medals and prizes. All of the medals, awards, and prizes are announced during an Awards Ceremony, which is the culmination of the Grand Jamboree.

Grand Jamboree

The Jamboree is an annual event that showcases work from the iGEM season. All participating iGEM teams are invited to present their synthetic biology projects and compete for prizes. This event is a celebration of synthetic biology accomplishments, featuring presentations, poster sessions, workshops, and social events.

Have a Question?

Check out the Frequently Asked Questions page to see if your question is answered there. If you cannot find the answer on that page, please email us hq AT igem DOT org and we’ll do our best to help!

Navigating iGEM

Starting an iGEM team and getting a project started can be a challenge even for returning teams. We have prepared this guide to help you understand what to expect throughout the iGEM Competition Cycle, whether this is your first time participating, or you are returning as a seasoned veteran.

Below you'll find some highlighted items that teams should be working on before and during the iGEM season. If you have any suggestions for content or questions, please send an email to us at hq [AT] igem [DOT] org!

The iGEM Cycle

Not all teams follow this cycle (for example, you may start work earlier or later than this shows), but this is the general flow of the major tasks involved with participating in iGEM.

The iGEM Cycle
Off Season Phase (December-January)

During the months of December through January, students and instructors who are interested in having an iGEM team often begin preparing for the upcoming season. Here are some ideas of what you can do during the iGEM Off Season:

If you’ve never competed in iGEM, reach out to the iGEM Ambassadors
Learn from experienced iGEMers how to start an iGEM team in your region. Contact your iGEM Ambassadors by email at ambassadors [AT] igem [DOT] org.
If you have competed in iGEM before, take time to debrief with your PI and team
Assess successes and failures, discuss interest in continuing next year, etc.
Raise awareness of iGEM at your school
Run informational sessions, post flyers, talk to Professors and fellow students
Find a PI to mentor your team and provide lab space
This may be a challenge if you're a first-time team, but stay positive! Make sure to talk to numerous Professors in your Biology, Biomedical Engineering, and Engineering departments and gauge interest early.
Recruit interested students
Advertise that you're looking to find interested students with flyers and/or recruit students from courses.
Start fundraising efforts (this should be an on-going effort throughout the season)
Email and call local companies, talk to the head of your department or college, investigate other on-campus funding sources like undergraduate research opportunity programs (UROP).

Phase 1 (February-May)
Start a Team
Decide the final team from the group of interested students.
Brainstorm Project Ideas
Ideally, iGEM projects are entirely conceived, led and driven by students. Many teams will have multiple brainstorming sessions, sometimes with and sometimes without the team instructors.
Determine Team Goals and Tasks
This can be challenging, but well worth the effort in the end. A list of high level goals and a detailed list of tasks needed to reach those goals will help your team stay focused on the project.
Read the Competition Requirements
Make sure that your team has covered all aspects of the requirements, deliverables, and deadlines, as some aspects may be very important to know before starting to plan your project. This should be the responsibility of the entire team and not the sole responsibility of one team member.

Phase 2 (June, July, and August)
Start Work on Project
For wet lab work, you should get trained in protocols and follow your school's safety regulations, and start working on your project. You must also follow iGEM’s safety rules and policies throughout your project.
Build Your Designs
Order your DNA, and assemble your genetic devices and systems.
Run Functional Experiments
This can be done throughout the cloning process once you have a functional device. Test your device and collect your data.
Process and Plot Your Results
Determine which software you need to use to process your data, determine the units you want to display, and make sure you include your controls during data analysis and show them on your graphs/plots.
Work on your Team Wiki and Registry Part pages
Good project documentation is essential for communicating your project to the world. You should be continually working on your Team Wiki and Registry Part pages throughout the project. Don't wait until the last minute!

Phase 3 (September, October)
Create Materials to Showcase Your Project
Once you have your final data plotted and ready to show, you should create materials to showcase your project at the Jamboree.
Finalize Your Project Documentation
Now is the time to finalize your documentation, including Team Wiki, Registry Part Pages, and Judging Form.
Prepare and Practice Your Presentation
Similar to your Team Wiki, you should work on your presentation all throughout the project. Practice your presentation in front of a live audience to develop your presentation skills, and integrate feedback to refine the content.
Attend the Grand Jamboree in Paris
Meet other iGEM teams, celebrate your work, and have fun!

“iGEM is not easy, but it’s worth it” is a refrain that you’ll hear from many an experienced iGEMer. As you and your teammates work through the phases of the iGEM cycle, you'll likely experience times filled with excitement and high energy that boost your confidence, along with periods of frustration and disappointment that challenge your motivation. Remember that both the highs and the lows are part of any creative process, and that both are necessary and worthy of celebration as part of your iGEM journey.

On this page

The iGEM Cycle
Off Season Phase (December-January)
Phase 1 (February-May)
Phase 2 (June, July, and August)

Budgeting and Fundraising

You’ve formed an iGEM team. You and your teammates have decided on a project. You’re excited about the journey that lies before you. Now is the time to share the joy and excitement of your iGEM project with those who can help support and fund your team. Here are some ideas and resources to help you put together a fundraising plan:

Step 1: Develop A Communication Strategy

The first step is getting together with your team and preparing your message. You need to think of a clear, concise way in which you can explain your project and why you need help funding it.

Begin by asking yourself the following questions:

Who are you?
What is your team’s project?
Why did you choose to work on this project?
What will you use the money for?
Why is the iGEM Competition important to you?

Once you have answered these questions for yourselves, think about how you will communicate with others. Presenting your project may be a balancing act; you will need to give the right amount of information so your audience can connect to what you are doing, but be careful to not overwhelm them. A simple and compelling idea can go very far.

Create a brochure

Consider creating a brochure in which you state the goals of your team. Having a team identity (logo, team color, presentation layout, etc.) will help you look professional and serious about what you are doing. For examples, take a look at the brochures created by these iGEM teams: Duke 2006; MIT 2008 page 1 ↗, page 2 ↗.

Record a promotional video

Recording your story in a short promotional video is not only a requirement for the iGEM Competition, it’s also a great tool for raising funds. Let your audience see who they are helping; it will be easier to relate to the project if they see who is working behind it. A promotional video is also useful if you plan to obtain money via an online crowd-funding platform. For inspiration, check out the iGEM 2020 ↗, iGEM 2021 ↗, iGEM 2022 ↗, and iGEM 2023 ↗ promotional videos.

Leverage your wiki

Remember that your wiki is also a valuable tool for fundraising. You can use it as your website to tell your story to the outside world. (You can also look at the wikis of previous iGEM teams in your region to identify sponsors who supported their projects.)

The first step is getting together with your team and preparing your message. You need to think of a clear, concise way in which you can explain your project and why you need help funding it.
Step 2: Create A Budget

Be sure to have a budget ready when you present your project to possible funding sources. This will help you explain why you need money and will give your team a serious and professional image. There are several things you need to consider for making your budget, some expenses vary from region and country. You can consult other iGEM teams from your area to get an idea of their budget.

Teams create a cohesive team identity through branded clothing, and giveaways like pins or stickers.
Team Fees

To participate in an iGEM Competition, teams should plan their budget to cover the required team fees:

Stage I - Registration, which officially launches your team’s participation in iGEM.

Stage II - Finalization, which confirms your team’s commitment to completion and enters your project into the final evaluation and judging phase of iGEM.

Grand Jamboree Tickets and Travel

The Grand Jamboree is taking place at the Porte de Versailles (Paris, France). Team members who wish to attend the Paris Jamboree in person must also purchase individual tickets to the event. You’ll also need to consider travel and accommodation costs for your team to stay in Paris.

Laboratory Expenses

Talk to your instructors and advisors regarding what lab materials are available at the institution. Some of these materials might already be in your school and can be provided for your team. Also consider reaching out to local biotech companies, they can provide you with lab materials such as lab gloves, pipettes and other useful supplies.

Regional Meetups

We encourage teams to get together and share their work at regional team meetups. These events can sometimes have associated expenses that would need to be included in the team budget. When travel is not possible, you may consider switching to an online virtual meetup.

Team Identity

We encourage you to create your own team branding material. Logos and mascots can be used to present your project to the rest of the world. Be as creative as you want! Some local companies can help by sponsoring the creation of these items so be sure to look for their support. Teams have designed logos, T-shirts, hoodies, vests, posters, mascots, cups, comic books, pins, stickers, and more!

Summer Stipends

Some iGEM teams help students who are working on the project to support themselves during the summer. This aid can take the form of funds to pay the student’s expenses, social service hours or professional service hours in some countries. Talk to the department heads at your institution to get the help you need to work full-time on your project.

Step 3: Approach Potential Funders

Once you have your communication strategy and budget, you can start approaching funding sources. You should aim for the majority of your team activities (participation, registration, lab work, stipends, etc.) to be funded by your university and your government. Gaining support from university or government officials is not just good for your team - it can help to build a lasting legacy for iGEM teams after you too!

Your School or University

The first place you should request funding is from your school or university. Try speaking with department heads, school deans, or work with your team PI to get iGEM into their budget and grant proposals. If there is not already a synthetic biology department, teams can seek support from biology, biochemistry, medical, and/or engineering departments.

Local or State Government

You may consider approaching your local, state, or federal government to set up funding for multiple iGEM teams in the region. See if your state or country has a bio-economy or synthetic biology strategy, or any initiatives focused on innovation and STEM.

Here are some other ways to get extra funding for your iGEM team:

Corporate Sponsorship

Companies may be interested in providing financial support to a team if the project aligns with their interests. Teams may offer brand exposure based on the level of financial support on places such as their wikis, T-shirts, or presentations. Put your team's branding and communication materials to work to tell a compelling story that will align with the company's interests.

In-Kind Sponsorship

Sometimes companies who are unable to provide financial support are more willing to provide in-kind sponsorship. This can come in the form of free access to their goods and services. This type of sponsorship can reduce laboratory-associated costs, and allow you to divert those funds to other aspects of your project.

Alumni Associations

Some educational institutions have alumni associations that can assist in providing funding for your project. Consider talking to your school's alumni association about this. We also encourage you to work with the iGEM alumni at your school and in your region. Contact iGEM Community (community AT igem DOT org) to be connected to past participants.

Crowdfunding

Creating a crowdfunding campaign online can help you easily receive donations from your community. Here are some crowdfunding examples: CampFire ↗ (Japan), Experiment.com ↗ (Global), FlipGive ↗ (North America), GoFundMe ↗ (Global), Indiegogo ↗ (Global), Ketto ↗ (India), Ulule.com ↗ (Global)

iGEM Partner Special Offers

The iGEM Foundation is supported by various sponsors. We work every year on getting special offers for our iGEM teams. Subscribe to the iGEM Newsletter ↗ to stay updated!

As you develop and implement your fundraising plan, we encourage you to be creative and not be afraid to try new ideas. Approach fundraising in the same way that you approach your iGEM project itself – design a fundraising plan, build your communication materials, test the effectiveness of your approach, and learn what worked and what you could do better. Discover insights and lessons learned by checking out the wikis of previous iGEM teams, and the guides put together by UMaryland 2015, CLSB-UK 2017, and IONIS-PARIS 2017 ↗.

Enjoy the fundraising experience – it’s a wonderful opportunity for you to engage your school and local community in learning about and sharing in the excitement of your iGEM project. And remember to thank those who support you. You’ll no doubt make connections, and even friends, that you’ll value long after the competition has ended.

Sponsors and Special Team Offers
Participating in the iGEM Competition unlocks access
to exclusive sponsorship offers for teams
As a non-profit organization, we count on the support of our partners and sponsors to help us continue to provide a unique and valuable experience to students worldwide. Thank you to all the sponsors who have helped make iGEM possible. We will update this page as more team sponsorships become available.
Learn more about sponsorships at iGEM ↗
Navigate to
2026
Partner Sponsors
Integrated DNA Technologies (IDT)
iGEM Sponsor since 2014
Platinum Partner Sponsor
40 kb of Free DNA Synthesis
ABOUT IDT
Integrated DNA Technologies (IDT) is a leader in the development and manufacture of products for research and diagnostic life sciences. As one of the world's largest suppliers of custom nucleic acids, IDT serves academic research, biotechnology, and pharmaceutical development communities with products that support applications including synthetic biology, next generation sequencing (NGS), gene amplification, SNP detection, expression profiling, and gene quantification. Platform-independent NGS products are available in addition to DNA and RNA oligonucleotides, qPCR assays, siRNA duplexes, gBlocks™ Gene Fragments for gene construction, and custom gene synthesis.
SPECIAL TEAM OFFER
Our partnership with IDT has transformed the way iGEMers around the world approach synthetic biology. Registered teams in the 2026 iGEM Competition can get up to 40 kb of custom DNA from IDT.
Learn more →
Last day to register: September 22, 2026
Twist Bioscience
iGEM Sponsor since 2015
Platinum Partner Sponsor
20 kb of Free DNA Synthesis
ABOUT TWIST
At Twist Bioscience, we work in service of customers who are changing the world for the better. In fields such as medicine, agriculture, industrial chemicals and data storage, by using our synthetic DNA tools, our customers are developing ways to better lives and improve the sustainability of the planet. The faster our customers succeed, the better for all of us, and Twist Bioscience is uniquely positioned to help accelerate their efforts.
SPECIAL TEAM OFFER
Twist Bioscience has teamed up with iGEM to offer every team complimentary synthesis of 20kb worth in Twist DNA, available as either Gene Fragments or Express Clonal Genes. iGEM teams can now save precious project time by ordering free synthetic DNA that arrives fast, ready to use, cloned into a Twist stock vector or an iGEM vector!

GenScript is a global leader in gene synthesis and life science services, dedicated to accelerating innovation in healthcare and biotechnology. Founded in 2002 in New Jersey, GenScript provides high-quality genes, peptides, proteins, and antibodies to support life science research, translational medicine, and early-stage drug development. As the largest gene provider since 2008, GenScript has consistently supported iGEM since 2009. We are committed to empowering synthetic biology innovation by delivering fast, reliable, and cost-effective DNA synthesis solutions to researchers around the world.

MathWorks is a leading developer of computational software. MATLAB is a programming environment for algorithm development, data analysis, visualization, and numerical calculations, while Simulink is a graphical environment for simulation and model-based design. Engineers and scientists worldwide use these products to accelerate research and innovation in biotechnology, pharmaceuticals, automotive, aerospace, electronics, financial services, and other industries. iGEM teams can use MATLAB and Simulink products to help organize, model, and develop complex biological systems, analyze and visualize data, interface with a variety of hardware, and more.

PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

This page contains the important dates for iGEM Teams. "Freeze" deadlines are hard deadlines that will not be extended. (No exceptions!) The time for each freeze is 15:00 UTC (Coordinated Universal Time) on the date indicated. The time shown on your device is the corresponding local time for you. If you have any questions regarding this Competition Calendar or the deadlines, please don't hesitate to contact iGEM HQ by emailing us at hq [AT] igem [DOT] org.

January
February
March
May
June
July
August
AUGUST
13
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Animal Use Forms Due

Teams must submit Animal Use Forms to request permission before working with animals or samples from animals (the Animal Use Policy covers vertebrates, like rats or fish, and some invertebrates, like octopus and bees). This is the last date to submit Animal Use Forms. Teams can submit Animal Use Forms at any time until this date.

Project Promotion Video Due

The Project Promotion Video is due. This video is the introduction to your project and should include: The problem you are addressing, your solution, the engineering involved and your project’s potential impact. This is your project’s pitch.

September
SEPTEMBER
3
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Final Project Safety Form Due

Your Project Safety Form must be completed and submitted by one of your team's PIs. If your PI cannot complete this form before the deadline, please email us at safety [AT] igem [DOT] org to tell us about your situation.

Check-in Forms Due

Teams must submit Check-in Forms to request permission before working with any organism, part or activity that is not on the White List. This is the last date to submit Check-in Forms. Teams can submit Check-in Forms at any time until this date.

SEPTEMBER
17
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Team Roster Freeze

The final version of your Team Roster is due. Please make sure that all team members, including students, instructors, advisors and PIs, are listed on the official Roster by this Roster Freeze. Only team members on the official roster will receive participation certificates.

Live Stage Talk Proposals Due

Live Stage Talks are an opportunity to present your team's project, work or special interests to the global audience at the Grand Jamboree. In-person teams can propose up to three Live Stage Talks until the deadline. Be advised that available slots are limited.

Team Booth Safety Proposals Due

There are restrictions on what can and cannot be brought to the Grand Jamboree. If you want to request special permission to bring equipment or material to the Jamboree, please review the Team Booth Requirements, and submit the required application form by the deadline.

October
OCTOBER
6
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Non-Deliverable Videos Submission

Non-deliverable videos are optional, creative videos that teams can showcase on their wiki to highlight their project, outreach, team activities, or other aspects of their iGEM experience. To include them, upload your videos to Video Universe by this deadline, following the submission instructions.

OCTOBER
8
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Freeze Day
Judging Form Freeze

To become eligible for medals and prizes, you must complete and submit your Judging Form by this deadline. Any member of the Team Roster may edit and submit this form. Failure to complete and submit the Judging Form prevents judges from evaluating your team for medals and/or prizes.

Project Attributions Form Freeze

Complete and submit your Project Attributions Form by this deadline. The standard form iframe provided on the Project Attributions page in your Team Wiki template must be left untouched.

Wiki Freeze

Your Team Wiki is a website that serves as the public face of your project. Project documentation must be completed on your Wiki by this deadline. Once the Wikis are frozen, no further edits will be allowed until after the Grand Jamboree has taken place. No extensions will be provided, as judges will begin their assessment directly after the Wiki Freeze. Make sure you begin work on it early, to avoid last-minute problems and mistakes.

Note: During the days just prior to the Wiki Freeze, iGEM experiences higher than normal traffic, which can lead to strain on our servers and sessions "timing out" for users. iGEM tries to alleviate this strain, but there may be over 7,000 users editing their pages during this period. DO NOT wait until the final days to work on or finish your Wiki. Edits made during the final week should only be very minor edits (i.e., fixing spelling mistakes, updating images, etc.).

Registry Part Pages Freeze

You are expected to submit any new Parts you create to the Registry of Standard Biological Parts. All Part information you wish to submit for judge evaluation must be documented on Registry Part Pages by this Part Pages Freeze. After the Registry of Standard Biological Parts is frozen, no further edits to Part Pages will be allowed until after the Grand Jamboree.

Project Software / GitLab Freeze

All teams working on Project Software, including teams competing in the Software & AI Village, or nominating themselves for Best Software Tool prize, must host the source code of their software on the dedicated repository on iGEM's GitLab by this deadline. After iGEM's GitLab is frozen, no further changes to Project Software will be allowed until after the Grand Jamboree.

OCTOBER
15
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Presentation Video Due

The Presentation Video is your opportunity to communicate the details of your project to a broad audience. Presentation Videos must be submitted by this deadline. Judges will begin their assessment of your Presentation Video once this deadline has passed. Please avoid submitting in the minutes just before this deadline, in case there are technical issues.

OCTOBER
28
00:00 (GMT)
EVENT
Grand Jamboree October 28-31

The iGEM Grand Jamboree returns to Paris this year. Join us at this celebration of synthetic biology in one of the most beautiful cities in the world! At the Grand Jamboree, the world's largest community of synbio researchers, industry executives, investors, startups and policymakers will come together, all working to build a better world, using synthetic biology.

During the Grand Jamboree, iGEM Competition teams will complete their Judging Sessions. In-person teams will also show off their projects at their Team Booths and Live Stage Talks. Finalists attending in person will be invited to the main stage to share their projects with the entire Grand Jamboree audience during the Final Day Show.

NOVEMBER
9
15:00 (GMT)
Wiki Thaw

Team Wikis will be thawed and opened for editing. You can use this time to update your team's results and awards, or make any project amendments.

December

Closing Date for the 2025 iGEM Competition / Wiki Archive Date

Team Wikis will be re-frozen and archived at this time. Before this deadline is your last chance to make final corrections, or to add your Jamboree results. You will not be able to edit your pages again after this date, so please ensure all of the content is as you want it and that it's correct!

iGEM's Technology Program

iGEM's Technology Program is developing new technology-related initiatives and resources, while also supporting existing iGEM ones, such as the iGEM Registry of Standard Biological Parts, the iGEM Distribution, and the resources and thrusts of the iGEM engineering committee.

Our purpose is to offer support to the entire iGEM community. This includes support for the current iGEM competition cycle and for the future development of the community and the research it collectively conducts. Our technology- related initiatives span from the creation and curation of online databases to resources for improving experimental standardization to the forming of strategic partnerships to further advance your ability to conduct synthetic biology research. You will find all of these resources within the subsections of the Technology site.

The iGEM Registry of Standard Biological Parts is a continuously growing community collection of biological parts that can be mixed and matched to build synthetic biology devices and systems. The Registry is an open community that runs and grows on the "Get & Give (& Share)" philosophy. Users get parts, samples, data, and tools from the Registry to work on their synthetic biology projects. They give back the new parts they have made, as well as data and experience on new and existing parts. This reciprocity has worked incredibly well, with users building on the work of previous users.

Where to get started

If you are new to synthetic biology, we recommend that you start by taking a look at our Engineering Resources section. This section includes information on the Design-Build-Test-Learn cycle found within synthetic biology, but also contains webinars spanning a large number of topics from project ideation to wet lab and dry lab work.

If you are here for our resources, please check out the appropriate subsection, whether it is the Parts Registry, the Distribution Kit, the current interlab procedures, mammalian or plant synthetic biology, or our mission statement and members of the Engineering Committee. Additional sections will be added as the Technology side of iGEM continues to grow!

name: engineering
title: Engineering | iGEM Technology
content:
Committee
Distribution
Registry
Technologies

Chassis
An Introduction to Engineering in Synthetic Biology

Synthetic biology is often described as an approach that uses engineering principles to design and build biological systems. Therefore, the engineering of a biological system (frequently bacterial), is one of the central components of nearly every iGEM project, and for those where it is not, the project frequently seeks to assist other members of the iGEM community who are working with biological systems. Here, you can find information on what engineering is, how best to approach the challenge of engineering biological systems, and how to determine if your engineering solution has been successful through qualitative assessments and more importantly quantitative measurements.

Questions?

If you have questions about engineering in Synthetic Biology, please email technology@igem.org ↗.

What is Engineering?

Engineering is really a set of principles that we use to efficiently design and build items of interest. As EngineerGirl ↗ puts it, when we say engineering principles, “we mean the ideas, rules, or concepts that need to be kept in mind when solving an engineering problem.” How an engineer approaches a problem is very different from how a scientist doing basic research or discovery research approaches a problem. As for what are those specific ideas, rules, or concepts that we need for our engineering problems, that will vary from one engineering problem to another. The set of principles needed for building a bridge or airplane are quite different from the set of principles required to build a bacterial cell that produces a drug compound or that can screen for disease.

For synthetic biology, we need to consider how to adapt many of these engineering principles for biological systems. Some of the more obvious ones might include: modular biological parts, blue prints in the forms of predictive biological modeling and the in silico design of DNA circuits, measurements of the performance and function of the biological parts and overall biological system, evaluating the design through data analysis, and then repeating this process through additional replicates or by tweaking the original design.

In all engineering fields: electrical, mechanical, software, and biological, we use these principles to design and build things. They help ensure that the end product meets the initial expectations, and that the development process is efficient in terms of money, time, and resources used. This process is explained below using the example of building an aircraft, which is very expensive and costly and can have very serious consequences if not done properly.

The Engineering Process

The first step of building an airplane is to define the design constraints and specifications based on its intended purpose which should be up to its future usage. This essentially describes what the aircraft should, and should not, do and is critical to ensure that the final product is fit for purpose. For example, if building a helicopter, the aircraft should be able to take off vertically, but this isn’t essential for an airplane.

The next step is to design a prototype for the aircraft which meets the specifications. Once a prototype has been designed, it needs to be tested. Unfortunately, it isn’t feasible to just build the aircraft and then see if it flies. Instead, the prototype is modeled and simulated in various ways to see whether it functions correctly. Based on model data, the prototype is then adjusted until it meets the specifications.

Following this, a plan for how to build the prototype is made. The build plan usually makes heavy use of standard components from previous aircraft which are known to work. Usually, the prototype is built in stages to test each part separately as this is less expensive than building an entire aircraft which may not function correctly.

Once the prototype (or part of the prototype) is built, it is tested. This test data is then compared to the models/simulations and the specifications, and if necessary, the initial design is modified. The process of modeling/simulating, building, and testing the new design is then performed again. This process is repeated until the aircraft prototype meets the specifications.

[Reference: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aircraft_design_process ↗]

The process described above of designing, building, testing, and then learning from the data collected is known as the Engineering Cycle.

The Engineering Cycle

Like other engineering fields, engineering a biological system is a complex, challenging, and comprehensive process that can move very slowly at times. It involves an engineering cycle consisting of the four stages below:

Design: Engineering principles are used to specify a biological system with an intended function, and models are used to help improve the initial design according to these principles.
Build: The desired DNA sequence encoding the biological system or a part of the biological system is constructed and introduced to a chassis, frequently the target organism.
Test: The function of the engineered biological system is assayed.
Learn: The discrepancies between the desired and observed function are analyzed, frequently with quantitative data, to develop improved models and design parameters.

This process is repeated until a biological system with the desired function is identified. This incremental and iterative approach enables engineering despite our (often largely) incomplete understanding of many biological processes.

This engineering cycle can also be applied to the development of models in the design stage.

To learn more about the various stages, please go to our Design Stage, Build Stage, Test Stage, and Learn Stage pages.

On this page

The Engineering Process
The Engineering Cycle
