name: judging_special_prizes
title: Special Prizes | iGEM Competition
content:
PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

Special Prizes recognize focused areas of excellence in iGEM projects. They are competitive prizes and are awarded to honor the teams with the most impressive work in specific elements of their projects.

Special Prize Winners

The Special Prize winners are the top scorers in every section for each prize. Thus, three of each kind of Special Prize are awarded: One for high school, one for undergraduate and one for overgraduate. The scores are determined from the corresponding ballot area. Teams must meet a minimum performance score for any prize to be awarded.

Keep reading to learn how Special Prizes are scored, and to discover the focus areas and requirements for each prize.

How Are Special Prizes Judged?
Scoring Special Prizes

To score teams, iGEM uses an academic rubric that provides common standards for all judges to use and communicates performance standards to teams. The ballot consists of specific questions for judges to answer. For each question, a judge must select a score from 1 - 6 based on six language choices that describe a range of the quality of the work. The scores judges can give roughly correspond to:

Poor work done or completely absent.
Less work done; multiple missing components.
Some work done; incomplete.
Good work; complete.
Great work; complete with some impressive components.
Amazing work; impressive.

The specific criteria for each question will not be shared here. We do want iGEMers to know they are being evaluated using standardized criteria. We expect to see lots of amazing and impressive work!

Judging Criteria

All Special Prizes are listed below with a brief description, their ballot questions and their corresponding Standard URL.

Ballot questions are the questions that a judge will score when evaluating a team’s work for special prizes.

Standard URLs are specific URL addresses on Team Wikis that are designated for documentation towards each judging criterion. When working with Parts, you must also document your work on the relevant Part Pages on the Registry. These URLs and/or Part Pages link the judge to your work through the Judging Form. Judges are not obligated to look beyond the Standard URLs or Part Pages for specific work. If you document work outside of these designated places, your work may not be evaluated. Learn more about Standard URLs on the Team Wiki deliverables page.

Note: The URLs listed below are for team "example". Please replace "example" with your team name to find the page on your Wiki.

Please also see the current Judging Handbook to learn more about each prize.

Standard Special Prizes

All teams are eligible for the Best Presentation and Best Wikis Prizes. The scores from these sections also contribute to the Grand Prizes and Village Awards.

Best Presentation

Presentations should be clear, engaging and should communicate your project to a scientific/ engineering community. Can your team tell your story and present your data in a concise and visually appealing way? See the Presentation Video page for requirements.

Standard URL: None. Judges will evaluate your Team Presentation Video.

Ballot Questions:

1

	

How well did the team communicate their project goals, design, and results with convincing evidence?

2

	

How well does this presentation convey what the team accomplished through their technical data or results (either modeled or experimentally measured)?

3

	

Does the visual design of the presentation support the communication of the technical content (project design, results and data analysis, and conclusions)?

4

	

Did you find the presentation engaging?

5

	

Were images, prior work, background information, and supporting data acknowledged appropriately?

Best Wiki

The Team Wiki is the “face” of your iGEM project. Team Wikis serve as the main project information resource for future iGEM students and teams, as well as for the rest of the world. This award honors the Team Wiki that exemplifies what the following year’s Wikis should strive for. See the Team Wiki page for requirements.

Standard URL: None. Judges will evaluate your entire Team Wiki.

Ballot Questions:

1

	

How well does the Wiki communicate the team's project and their goals?

2

	

Did the team clearly document their project and support their results with convincing, experimental evidence?

3

	

Does the design of the Wiki support visitors in finding and understanding the content?

4

	

Will the Wiki be a compelling record of the team's project for future teams?

5

	

Is the content (including text, images, and prior work from iGEM teams and other research groups) on the Wiki properly referenced and cited?

Elective Special Prizes

In addition to eligibility for the Best Wiki and Presentation Prizes, teams can elect to compete for up to three additional Special Prizes. The limit is intended to encourage teams to focus on quality over quantity in their efforts to excel in the Competition.

Elective Special Prizes are grouped into two categories: General Biological Engineering and Specialization. Teams must choose at least one Special Prize from the General Biological Engineering list and one from the Specializations list. The third prize can be chosen from either list. Teams will designate the prizes they want to be considered for on the Judging Form.

The work done for these elective special prizes is judged in two different and independent ways.

To earn a gold medal teams must impress the judges on work towards each of their three selected prizes. Read more about medal judging here.
To win the specific Special Prize teams must score the highest within their section on the relevant ballot questions. These ballot questions are listed below.
General Biological Engineering
Best Measurement

Measurements are critical to scientific communication and advancement. Well-reported measurements are the only way to show whether hardware is functioning correctly, whether data are reliable and whether a result is actually important. There is high value in identifying appropriate targets for measurement, collecting precise measurements, and reporting results clearly and with appropriate units. Document your careful measurement efforts and you could win this award!

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/measurement

Ballot Questions:

1

	

Could the measurement(s) be repeated by other iGEM teams?

2

	

Is the protocol described well?

3

	

Could it be useful to other projects?

4

	

Did the team appropriately use controls to validate the measurement process and calibrate units?

Best Model

Models and computer simulations can help us understand the function and operation of BioBrick Parts and Devices. Simulation and modeling are critical engineering skills that can contribute to project design or provide a better understanding of the modeled process. These processes are even more useful and/or informative when real-world data are included in the model. This award is for teams who build a model of their system and use it to inform system design or simulate expected behavior before, or in conjunction with, experiments in the wetlab.

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/model

Ballot Questions:

1

	

How impressive is the modeling?

2

	

Did the model help the team understand a part, device or system?

3

	

Did the team use measurements of a part, device or system to develop the model?

4

	

Does the modeling approach provide a good example for others?

Best New Basic Part

Most genetically-encoded functions have not yet been converted to BioBrick parts. Thus, there are many opportunities to find new, cool, and important genetically encoded functions, and refine and convert the DNA encoding these functions into BioBrick standard biological parts. To be eligible for this award, this part must consist of a single genetic component and be documented on the Main Page on the Part's Page on the Registry. Winning parts are highly documented with experimental details and data showing the part working. See parts BBa_K863006 and BBa_K863001 as examples.

Standard URL: None. Judges will evaluate the documentation on the Part Page in the Registry for the designated part. You will enter the part number on the Judging Form for this prize. Although not required, Teams can add additional pages to their Wiki to describe their best parts and how they fit into their project.

Ballot Questions:

1

	

How does the documentation on the Registry compare to BBa_K863006 and BBa_K863001?

2

	

Did the team show the part works as expected (modeling data can be acceptable)?

3

	

Could it be useful to the community?

4

	

How well characterized (experimentally measured or modeled) is this Basic Part when tested in a device?

Best New Composite Part

New BioBrick devices can be made by combining existing BioBrick Parts. For example, Inverters, Amplifiers, Smell Generators, Protein Balloon Generators, Senders, Receivers, Actuators, and so on. To be eligible for this award, this part will consist of a combination of genetic components and be documented on the Main Page on the Part's Page on the Registry. Winning parts are highly documented with experimental details and data showing the part working. See parts BBa_K404122 and BBa_K863005 as examples.

Standard URL: None. Judges will evaluate the documentation on the Part Page in the Registry for the designated part. You will enter the part number on the Judging Form for this prize. Although not required, Teams can add additional pages to their Wiki to describe their best parts and how they fit into their project.

Ballot Questions:

1

	

How does the documentation on the Registry compare to BBa_K404122 and BBa_K863005?

2

	

Did the team show the part works as expected (modeling data can be acceptable)?

3

	

Could it be useful to the community?

4

	

How well characterized (experimentally measured or modeled) is this Composite Part?

Best New Improved Part

The Registry is rich with engineering opportunities. Can you redesign an existing part to create a new part with improved function? Can you show the improvement in an experiment by using the original part as a control? To be eligible for this award, this part must be well documented on the Main Page on the Part's Page on the Registry. Winning parts are highly documented with experimental details and data showing the new part works better than the old part, when measured in the same experiment. See this pair of parts as an example: existing part from 2016 (BBa_K2116002) and improved part from 2022 (BBa_K4387000).

Standard URL: None. Judges will evaluate the documentation on the Part Page in the Registry for each designated part. Make sure each Part Page (the new, improved part and the existing part) are well documented and reference each other. You will enter the part number of the New, Improved Part on the Judging Form for this prize. Although not required, Teams can add additional pages to their Wiki to describe their best parts and how they fit into their project.

Ballot Questions:

1

	

How does the documentation on the Registry for both the existing and improved parts compare to BBa_K2116002 (existing part) and BBa_K4387000 (improved part)?

2

	

Did the team show that the improved part functions better than the old part? (i.e., the old part was used as a control in the testing of the new part.)

3

	

Could the improved part be useful to the iGEM community?

4

	

How well characterized (experimentally measured or modeled) is this Improved Part?

Best Part Collection

Did your team make a collection of parts that work well together? For example, does your collection of parts have a coherent function, such as a modular sensing system or a tuneable production process? Do your parts function as a system that can be used or modified by other teams? If so, you should consider selecting Best Part Collection as one of your Special Awards for the Gold Medal!

New in 2025: Document Your Collection in the new Registry

You can now create your Part Collection directly in the new Registry. This feature allows you to provide documentation at the collection level, in addition to the existing documentation at the individual part level.

Collection documentation should describe the collection as a whole: what it does, how the parts work together, and how other teams can use or adapt it.

Part documentation should still be completed for every part in the collection. Each part’s Registry page must clearly describe its sequence, function, characterization, and usage. This way, you avoid duplicating text across multiple part pages while still giving judges a clear and focused view of both the system and its components.

What you need to do:

Create your Part Collection in the new Registry.
Document the collection itself on the collection page: purpose, design, system-level functionality, and integration.
On the Judging Form, provide the identifier of your part collection.

Check out the Judging Handbook for all part collection requirements.

Standard URL: None. Judges will read the Part Collection Documentation on the Registry to evaluate your collection.

Ballot Questions:

Is this collection a coherent group of parts meant to be used as a collection, or just a list of all the parts the team made?
How does the documentation compare to the BBa_K747000-095 collection?
Did the team finish building a functional system using this collection?
Can it be useful to the community?
Specializations
Best Education

Innovative educational tools and outreach activities have the ability to establish a two-way dialogue with new communities by discussing public values and the science behind synthetic biology. How have you developed new opportunities to include more people in shaping synthetic biology? Education activities do not have to be directly related to your project but may look at wider issues of iGEM or synthetic biology. Education activities must promote scientific learning and avoid simply proselytizing or marketing synthetic biology and/or iGEM. Document your approach, and what was learned by everyone involved, to compete for this award.

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/education

Ballot Questions:

How well did their work promote mutual learning and/or a dialogue?
Is it documented in a way that others can build upon?
Was it thoughtfully implemented?
Did the team convince you that their activities would enable more people to shape, contribute to, and/or participate in synthetic biology?
Best Entrepreneurship

The Best Entrepreneurship Prize recognizes exceptional effort to build a business case and commercialize an iGEM project. This award can go to a new project, or to a previous project that a team has aimed to commercialize. Successful teams will (1) construct a business plan based on customer needs and expert knowledge on feasibility and (2) create a minimum viable product.

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/entrepreneurship

Ballot Questions:

Has the team identified their first potential customers and their unmet need(s) that are not yet covered by existing solutions?
Has the team shown that their solution is possible, scalable and inventive?
Has the team presented logical product development plans with realistic milestones, timelines, resources and risks?
Has the team outlined the skills, capabilities, and the stakeholders required to be credible in developing their solution further?
Has the team considered the positive and negative long-term impacts of their fully developed solution?
Best Hardware

This is a prize for the team that has developed a piece of hardware for synthetic biology. Hardware in iGEM should make synthetic biology based on standard parts easier, faster, better or more accessible to our community. Did your team make a sensor to help teams characterize parts? Did you make a robot that can help teams perform experiments or do cloning more easily? Strong competitors for this prize will demonstrate utility, user testing, and easy reproducibility. Tell us what your team developed for this award!

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/hardware

Ballot Questions:

Does the hardware you developed address a need or problem in synthetic biology?
Did the team conduct user testing and learn from user feedback?
Did the team demonstrate utility and functionality in their hardware proof of concept?
Is the documentation of the hardware system sufficient to enable reproduction by other teams?
Inclusivity Award

The Inclusivity Award recognizes exceptional efforts to include people with diverse identities in scientific research. Who is allowed to have a voice in iGEM, synthetic biology and science more broadly? How have you developed new opportunities to eliminate barriers and allow more people to contribute to, participate in and/or be represented by these communities? To compete for this prize, activities do not have to be directly related to your team’s project. Document your approach, how you improved inclusivity and what was learned.

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/inclusivity

Ballot Questions:

How well did the work investigate barriers to participation in synthetic biology and/or science more broadly?
How well did the work expand access to synthetic biology and/or science more broadly?
Was there a dialogue with members of the target group, and were their needs, opinions and values considered?
Is the work documented in a way that other teams or external entities can build upon?
Best Integrated Human Practices

How does your project affect society and how does society influence the direction of your project? How might ethical considerations and stakeholder input guide your project purpose, design and the experiments you conduct in the lab? How did this feedback enter into the process of your work throughout the iGEM Competition? Document a thoughtful and creative approach to exploring these questions and how your project evolved in the process, to compete for this award.

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/human-practices

Ballot Questions:

How well was the Human Practices work integrated throughout the project?
How inspiring an example is it to others?
To what extent is the Human Practices work documented so that others can build upon it?
How thoughtfully was it implemented? How well did they explain the context, rationale and prior work?
How well did it incorporate different stakeholder views?
Best Plant Synthetic Biology

This award is designed to celebrate exemplary work done in plant synthetic biology. Did you build a project in a plant chassis? Did you submit plant parts to the Registry? This award could also be given to a team working with algae or another eukaryotic photosynthetic chassis. Show us what you made and remember to adhere to iGEM safety guidelines! Note: Photosynthetic bacteria cannot be considered for this prize.

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/plant

Ballot Questions:

How successful was the team in engineering a plant, protoplast or algal cell?
Does their work address a need or problem in plant synthetic biology?
Did the team create resources/parts/solutions that were suitable for and dependent upon plants/algae to function?
Could the parts/tools/protocols for plants/algae made during this project be useful to other teams?
Safety and Security Award

Synthetic biology will need to be used safely and securely if local people are to solve local problems all around the world. The Safety and Security Committee is challenging teams to apply biological engineering approaches to manage risks associated with synthetic biology. Can you take the next step in progress towards knowledge, understanding, and tools that will make the use of synthetic biology safer and more secure?

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/safety-and-security

Ballot Questions:

Did the team make a contribution to biosafety and/or biosecurity?
Is their contribution well characterized and/or well validated?
Did the team build upon existing knowledge, understanding, tools or approaches?
In addition to broader safety work, has the team managed risks from their project appropriately?
Has the team addressed the use of synthetic biology in the real world?
Best Software Tool

Regardless of what the topic is, iGEM projects often create or adapt computational tools to move their overall project forward. Because they are born out of a direct practical need, these software tools (or new computational methods) can even prove surprisingly useful for others. Without necessarily being big or complex, they can make the crucial difference to a project's success. This award tries to find and honor such "nuggets" of computational work. To be eligible, your software has to be documented and made available under an OSI-approved open-source license.

Two items of note:

Software & AI Village teams are not eligible for this special prize.
Teams applying for this prize MUST host the source code of their software on the dedicated repository on iGEM's GitLab. Learn about software requirements on the Software Project page.

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/software

Ballot Questions:

How well is the software compatible with, and does it leverage, existing synthetic biology standards? (e.g. SBOL, other RFCs, data formats, etc.)
Was this software validated by experimental work?
Can the software be useful to other projects?
How well can the software be integrated with external tools/software applications? (APIs, packages, etc.)
Is the software user-friendly?
How well is the software written and documented for future groups to extend and improve?(Code documentation, comments in the code, performance evaluation, architecture diagrams, installation and execution scripts, etc.)
Best Sustainable Development Impact

The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) are a call to action to integrally address global environmental, social, and economic challenges. iGEM teams can answer this call with this prize. Demonstrate how you have evaluated your project ideas against one or more of the SDGs, how you’ve consulted with SDG stakeholders, and how you’ve begun to form collaborations with other iGEM teams around the SDGs. You’re encouraged to look back at previous iGEM projects to evaluate them against the SDGs and build upon them.

Standard URL: Judges will evaluate: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/sustainability

Ballot Questions:

Did the team incorporate feedback from relevant Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) stakeholders into their work?
Did the team address potential long-term social, environmental and economic impacts of their work (in the context of the SDG(s) they have chosen)?
How well has the team considered the positive and/or negative interactions of their work in relation to other SDGs?
Has the team documented their work against their chosen SDG(s) so that other teams can build upon their work?
Has the team’s work measurably and significantly addressed one or more SDGs?

Questions? Ask on either the #⁠judging-hs or #judging-collegiate channels of the iGEM 2025 Global Slack.

name: deliverable_wiki
title: Team Wiki | iGEM Competition
content:
PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

Team Wiki

Your Team Wiki is a website you use to communicate your entire project to the world. This includes providing background information, describing project goals, and showing experimental results. The Team Wiki is a key deliverable for the iGEM Competition. It will be archived at the end of the year so that future teams and iGEM community members can see your work.

Questions? Ask on the #wiki channel of the iGEM 2025 Global Slack.

Deadlines
OCTOBER
6
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Non-Deliverable Videos Submission

Non-deliverable videos are optional, creative videos that teams can showcase on their wiki to highlight their project, outreach, team activities, or other aspects of their iGEM experience. To include them, upload your videos to Video Universe by this deadline, following the submission instructions.

OCTOBER
8
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Wiki Freeze

Project documentation must be completed on your Wiki by this deadline. Once the Wikis are frozen, no further edits will be allowed until after the Grand Jamboree has taken place. No extensions will be provided, as judges will begin their assessment directly after the Wiki Freeze. Make sure you complete work on it early, to avoid last-minute problems and mistakes.

Note: On the days just prior to the Wiki Freeze, iGEM experiences higher than normal traffic, which can lead to strain on our servers and sessions "timing out" for users. iGEM tries to alleviate this strain, but there may be over 7,000 users editing their pages during this period. Please DO NOT wait until the final days to work on or finish your Wiki. Edits made during the final week should only be very minor edits (i.e., fixing spelling mistakes, updating images, etc.).

NOVEMBER
9
15:00 (GMT)
Wiki Thaw

Team Wikis will be “thawed” and open for editing. You can use this time to update your team's results and awards, or make any project amendments.

DECEMBER
10
15:00 (GMT)
Closing Date for the 2025 iGEM Competition / Wiki Archive Date

Team Wikis will be re-frozen and archived at this time. Your last chance to make final corrections, or to add your Jamboree results, is prior to this deadline. You will not be able to edit your pages again after this, so please ensure all of the content is as you want it and that it’s correct!

Requirements

All teams must follow these requirements. Failure to follow the requirements may prevent your team from winning medals or prizes.

1. GitLab Repository Link

On every page, teams must add a clear and visible link to the team iGEM GitLab repository (e.g., gitlab.igem.org/2025/example ↗ for Example team) in the footer. This is to ensure that future teams and judges can easily find the source code that generated the Wiki.

2. Content Ownership And Licensing

All content created by iGEM teams and hosted on iGEM's websites must be available under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 ↗ license (or any later version). This license must be displayed in the footer of every Wiki page. The original LICENSE file on the team's Wiki repository must remain unmodified.

Third party assets, such as copyrighted images, fonts, and other materials, are generally not allowed on the Wiki unless they meet the following conditions:

The assets must have a license that explicitly allows reuse, redistribution, and modification (e.g. open-source code, Creative Commons licenses, etc.), allowing the asset to be uploaded to iGEM servers. Only assets with appropriate licenses that allow this are acceptable.
All assets must be uploaded directly to iGEM’s servers. External linking to assets hosted on third-party servers is not permitted.

All content (text, images, prior work from iGEM teams and other research groups) must be properly attributed, referenced or cited.

3. Standard URL Pages: Prizes And Medals Eligibility

For judges to find your work, you must use the Standard URL Pages. These pages have specific URL addresses designated for each judging criterion and are automatically linked to your team's Judging Form. If your documentation for an award is not on the page encoded by the static link, your team may not be judged for that prize.

What does this mean? Regardless of how you style your Wiki, you will need to preserve the designated URLs in order to be evaluated for the awards listed below. Beware! Web design packages that create their own dynamic links will not work!

Below are links to the team "example" Standard URL pages for various judging criteria. For your own pages, please replace "example" with your team name to find the page on your Wiki, or navigate to that page using the menu in your Team Wiki.

Standard URL Pages for Medal Criteria

Standard URL Pages for Special Prizes

Awards with no required Standard Page

The judging criteria are described in full detail in the Judging Pages.

4. All Content Must Be Hosted On iGEM Servers

All team pages, images, documents, code, including CSS, JavaScript and font files, must be hosted on iGEM servers (any subdomain of igem.org or igem.wiki):

HTML and all types of source code written by teams must be committed to the Team Wiki repository on the iGEM GitLab ↗.
Images, additional documents (.pdf, .csv, etc.) and fonts, must be uploaded to iGEM's own CDN static.igem.wiki using tools.igem.org/uploads.
Videos must be uploaded to the iGEM Video Universe and embedded from there. Please follow these instructions.
iGEM Foundation content hosted on igem.org can also be used on Team Wikis.

Why? We preserve all iGEM content on our own servers, so future teams can learn from what you have done. When you store content on external sites or servers, links might become broken, sites might go down and information is likely to be lost.

Use the External Content Check tool to verify that all content and requests on your Wiki follow this requirement. Failing this check disqualifies your team from Best Wiki and Best Software Tool Awards.

Judges will only evaluate content hosted on iGEM servers. Furthermore, displaying content hosted on external servers or CDNs, including external font providers, is strictly prohibited. This is considered a form of cheating and may result in disqualification from medals.

Note for package managers: The preferred way for using libraries is through package managers (npm, yarn, pip). Behind the scenes, package managers download the libraries, build the team Wiki and publish the resulting assets on our servers.

5. iframes Can Only Be Used To Show Content Hosted On iGEM Servers

You may use iframes to display a file that you have uploaded to iGEM servers (igem.org or igem.wiki) or the iGEM Video Universe (video.igem.org). iframes are not permitted on the iGEM Wiki to display content from another website or server (see above). Some common examples that are NOT permitted in an iframe include videos hosted on YouTube or Bilibili, and documents hosted on Google Drive or Microsoft Teams.

Note: Using iframes to show any non-iGEM server content is a form of cheating and will result in medal disqualification for your team.

6. All Source Code Used To Generate Your Wiki Must Be Provided In Your GitLab Repository

All source code used to generate your Wiki must be provided in your GitLab repository, including a properly configured CI/CD pipeline. The build process for your Wiki must be automated and executed via a .gitlab-ci.yml file in your repository.

Teams must not upload pre-generated HTML, CSS, or JavaScript files directly. Instead, the Wiki should be built from the source code using the CI/CD pipeline when deployed.

If you choose to use a custom HTML, CSS or JS generator instead of the default template, your GitLab repository must include a .gitlab-ci.yml file that automates the Wiki build process. This enables judges, other teams and anyone interested, to easily regenerate your Wiki using the source code in the future.

Recommendations
Explore GitLab Tools

GitLab is a powerful project management platform with many tools. Teams are encouraged to use Issues ↗, Boards ↗, Merge Requests ↗ and code review ↗ to organise, plan and distribute their tasks.

URL Naming Conventions

iGEM has adopted the following URL naming convention for better usability: - URLs should be in lowercase. - All characters except 0-9 and a-z should be replaced with -. - No leading or trailing -. e.g. /-page-name or /page-name-. - For example, the old format 2021.igem.org/Team:TeamName/Page_Name would become 2025.igem.wiki/team-name/page-name.

Using Software Tools Or Other Git Repositories

If you are using a separate program or software tool to create your Wiki pages (e.g. Github repository), we highly advise that you test importing the code over to your Team Wiki repository as early as possible. When you import pages that have been designed via other programs or that were hosted on other git platforms, it often takes time and effort to understand how to bring the correct files over. Do not wait until the week of the Wiki Freeze to test this! You may experience serious problems, and it may take longer than you expect to upload and build your website properly.

Examples

Heidelberg

2024.igem.wiki

JU-Krakow

2024.igem.wiki

Marburg

2024.igem.wiki

GEMS-Taiwan

2024.igem.wiki

ShanghaiTech-China

2023.igem.wiki

Leiden

2023.igem.wiki

ZJU-China

2023.igem.wiki

BASIS-China

2023.igem.wiki

Frequently Asked Questions

If you have any questions that are not answered here, do not hesitate to send them to the iGEM 2025 Global Slack in the #wiki channel.

Can I change my Wiki template later?
I made modifications in the code, but the Wiki stayed the same. Why?
Can I use this library/framework that everyone is talking about?
Can I call my backend APIs (for ChatBot or web server) in the Wiki?
The pipeline fails with “ERROR: Uploading artifacts as archive to coordinator. 413 Request Entity Too Large.”
The pipeline fails with “ERROR: jinja2.exceptions.TemplateSyntaxError: Unexpected end of template. Jinja was looking for the following tags: 'endblock'. The innermost block that needs to be closed is 'block'.”
How To Edit Your Wiki
Step 1: Activate Your Wiki

Start by activating your Wiki with one of three Wiki templates. You can select a template based on your team's web development skill level.

Go to teams.igem.org-> Select your team -> Scroll to "Team Deliverables Overview" -> Click the "Deliverables Dashboard" button. Note: Please log in to access the deliverables dashboard.
Scroll to "Team Wiki" and click the green arrow in the right-hand column of the table.
Select your template:
Markdown (Beginner)
HTML (Intermediate)
React (Advanced)
Click "Confirm" to activate your Team Wiki.

Once activated, you will find your Team Wiki at the following URL: https://2025.igem.wiki/team-name, with "team-name" being your registered team name (ex: Team Example's URL would be: https://2025.igem.wiki/example).

Team List
Step 2: Start Editing

Templates are just a starting point. You can modify the code to suit your team's needs. Feel free to explore the code and make changes to the design, layout and content.

Go to gitlab.igem.org ↗ -> Sign in using your iGEM Account.
Select your team from the list to see your team's Wiki repository. Note: You must be an official member of the Team Roster to edit your team's Wiki.
Please visit the iGEM GitLab Guide for more detailed instructions on how to edit your Team Wiki:
iGEM GitLab Guidetools.igem.org

iGEM GitLab

Shortcuts
Accessing iGEM GitLab
Quick Start for Given Templates
Managing Pages Based on Two Frozen Flask Templates
Video
Community Workshop: Hacking iGEM - GitLab

name: deliverable_part_pages
title: Registry Submissions | iGEM Competition
content:
PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

Registry Part Pages

iGEM maintains the Registry of Standard Biological Parts, which houses a growing collection of genetic parts that can be mixed and matched to build synthetic biology devices and systems. During the Competition, iGEM Teams have access to parts, data, and tools on the Registry to help them work on their synthetic biology projects.

One of the expectations for participating iGEM Teams is that they will submit any new parts they create to the Registry. This creates a record of the work they’ve done with their parts and helps the Registry grow. Teams are also encouraged, through the medal system, to characterize their new parts along with existing parts from the Registry, and to document that data on the Registry in order to help future teams use those parts. This expectation is a part of our "Get and Give" philosophy, where iGEM Teams receive existing parts through the iGEM Distribution Kit, and, in turn, they contribute new biological parts to the Registry.

Deadline
OCTOBER
8
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Registry Part Pages Freeze

Teams are expected to submit any new Parts to the Registry of Standard Biological Parts. To be entitled for judge evaluation, all part information must be documented on the part's page by this deadline.

Requirements
1. Part Submission to the iGEM Registry

Teams designing new DNA parts must add and document the new parts on the new iGEM Registry. Follow the step-by-step guides on How to Submit a Part and How to Create a Part Collection. Note: Parts with the status “Draft” will not be evaluated by judges. Make sure you send your parts to safety screening to publish them.

Teams working on existing parts should create a Documentation for each existing part.

Ensure your parts meet the Medal Criteria and any relevant Special Prizes.

2. Assembly Compatibility

Parts must conform to at least one of these standards:

BioBrick Standard (see RFC 10)
Type IIS Standard (see RFC 1000)

For sequence compatibility details and tools, visit: parts.igem.org/Help:Assembly_Compatibility

3. Documentation

Part documentation, including relevant design and experimental data, must be added to the Part's documentation section on the iGEM Registry. Judges will not evaluate part documentation on other pages, including your Team Wiki or any external sites.

Waiting until the end of the season can result in poorly documented pages and a variety of mistakes.

4. Authorship

The new Registry supports a full list of authors per part, each with their own institutional or team affiliation. However, when submitting a part for medal criteria or special prizes, all authors must share the same team affiliation. This ensures a part is not entered by multiple teams for the same award.

Recommendations

As you make new Part Pages, think carefully about the details you would need in order to fully understand and use the part in a project. All of the information needed to use the part successfully should be included on your Part Pages. Think back to a part you used from the Registry - were you able to use it easily? Or was more information required for ease of use? You should aim to create new Part Pages that allow future teams to use your part easily and successfully.

Here are some suggested questions to consider as you document your part(s):

Why is this part important and how can it be used?
How did you design this part? Did you review previous work (scientific literature, previous iGEM teams, etc.)?
Where did you obtain this DNA (DNA synthesis, PCR amplification, requested from a collaborator, etc.)?
Did you encounter problems with obtaining and/or using this part?
What level of biosafety does a laboratory need to have in order to use this part?
Do you have recommendations for testing your part, such as strains or cell lines?
What experiment did you run to test your part and what controls did you use?
What data did you generate while testing or using this part?

Do you need help designing your parts or experiments?

Check out technology.igem.org for resources to help you engineer your projects.

How to Add and Document Parts

The new iGEM Registry simplifies the process of adding and documenting parts. Follow the step-by-step guide on How to Submit a Part.

Note: Parts with the status “Draft” will not be evaluated by judges. Make sure you send your parts to safety screening to publish them.

How to Add Part Documentation

In your project, you will likely work with a mix of new parts you design this year and existing parts from the Registry. It’s important to document all the parts you use, whether they were created by your team or by others in previous years. For example, you might work with a well-established part like BBa_B0034 or a novel part introduced just last year. If your experience with that existing part would prove useful for future users of that part, you should document this on that Registry Part Page as a Documentation.

Go to the relevant part page on the new iGEM Registry. Note: Make sure you are logged in, and have opted in to be an author for the new iGEM Registry.
Scroll down to "Documentation".
Click "Create" on the right hand side.
Enter your data, then click the "Save" button to maintain your data.

Note: Only one person at a time should edit a Part Page. If multiple people are editing the same page at the same time, information may be lost!

name: deliverable_judging_form
title: Judging Form | iGEM Competition
content:
PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

Judging Form

To become eligible for medals and awards, your team must submit a Judging Form. This form will communicate your team's achievements to the judges. The form will include the Standard URLs to link the judges to the work you have done towards the medal criteria and the special prizes that you select.

When judges evaluate your team, they will look at your Team Wiki, watch your Presentation Video, and ask you questions during your Judging Session at the Grand Jamboree. Information from all of these is used during your evaluation.

Deadline
OCTOBER
8
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Judging Form Freeze

To become eligible for medals and prizes, you must complete and submit your Judging Form. Any member of the Team Roster may edit and submit this form. Failure to complete and submit the Judging Form prevents judges from evaluating your team for medals and/or prizes.

Requirements
You must use the correct Standard URL Pages on your Team Wiki to document your project. Do not use redirects to bypass the Standard URLs.
If your team works with Parts, you must create Registry Part Pages for all of the Parts that you intend to submit for medal criteria and for part awards. On your Judging Form, enter the relevant Part number in the appropriate box and verify that the linked Registry page documents your work correctly. Note: Do not leave your parts as drafts. Send your Parts to safety screening to publish them before filling out your team's Judging Form. Parts with the status “Draft” will not be evaluated by judges.
Software & AI Village teams, teams competing for the Best Software Tool prize, and any teams creating new software tools, must host the source code of the software on the team's dedicated repository in iGEM's GitLab ↗.
Recommendations

Before editing and submitting your Judging Form:

Read all the Medal Criteria.
Read the rules for Special Prizes.
Read the instructions on how to submit and document your Parts on the Registry.
Read the information about Standard URL Pages on the Team Wiki Requirements.
Title And Abstract

The Judging Form will prompt you to enter a title and abstract for your project. The title and abstract should provide a brief and accurate summary of the work you completed in your project. It will appear in the Grand Jamboree program and on your team information page for the public to view, as well as for judges. Each team should provide a project title of up to 15 words and a project abstract of up to 150 words. These should accurately reflect the team project and should summarize the work done in a clear, concise manner.

You can view some examples of titles and abstracts below:

2024 teams
2023 teams
2022 teams ↗
2020 teams
2019 teams
Start As Early As Possible

Do not wait until the day of the freeze to start working on your Judging Form. As soon as you have written your title and abstract, and you know which special prizes you want to compete for, you can fill out this form. Completing the form early means you can focus more on documenting your project as the Wiki Freeze deadline approaches.

How To Submit Your Judging Form

Deliverables Dashboard

Edit your Judging Form through your team's Deliverables Dashboard.

Navigate to the team list and select your team.
Scroll down to the “Team Deliverables Overview” section and click the “Deliverables Dashboard” button.
Locate “Judging Form” in the table and click on it to proceed.
Click on "Edit" to begin your Judging Form.
Click on "Save Draft" to maintain form information, and on "Submit Judging Form" to finalize your form submission.

Team List

name: deliverable_judging_session
title: Judging Session | iGEM Competition
content:
PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

Judging Session

The main judging activity at the Jamboree will be the Judging Session. This is your opportunity to have in-depth question and answer (Q&A) time with your judging panel. Prior to the Judging Session, your judges will watch your presentation video and review your Team Wiki. At the Judging Session, each team will be given five minutes to deliver an overview of the project and/or to describe any updates that occurred since the Team Wiki and presentation video were submitted. The rest of the session will be dedicated to discussion with the judges.

Since team members and judges may participate remotely, the Judging Sessions are designed to connect in-person participants to remote participants. Judging Sessions will take place physically at the Paris Convention Centre inside judging rooms. Each judging room will be equipped to run the meetings concurrently through Zoom. Thus, all teams must use Zoom to connect during the Judging Session, regardless of whether they are attending in person or remotely.

Deadlines
JULY
31
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Team Grand Jamboree Fee Due

Submit your Team Grand Jamboree Fee by this deadline to secure a Judging Session. Team members may also purchase tickets to attend the Jamboree in Paris in person. This is required to attend in person, in addition to the Team Fee.

OCTOBER
28
00:00 (GMT)
EVENT
Grand Jamboree October 28-31

Teams will complete their Judging Session during the Grand Jamboree. Each team will be randomly assigned a time for their Judging Session. Assignments are out now on the Jamboree website!

Format

Judging Sessions are split into 14 parallel sessions over the Jamboree competition days. They allow an in-depth discussion of the Team’s work with the judges. Judges will review team presentation videos and Wikis ahead of the Judging Session, and use the full 25-minute Judging Session to ask all of their questions.

Judging Sessions are hybrid. Each Judging Session room will host a Zoom meeting to allow judges and team members who are not in Paris to participate in the session remotely.

Judging Session rooms are closed, meaning only team members (which includes anyone on your Team Roster) and judges are allowed inside the judging room or in the online Zoom meeting.

Judging Sessions are live-streamed and recorded for online viewing and archiving purposes.

At your Judging Session, you can expect the following:

A 5-minute introduction delivered by your team.
20 minutes of question & answer time directly with your judges.
5 minutes of turnover time to the next team.

All sessions will begin and end on time. Late starts or extended sessions are not allowed, no matter the reason.

Requirements
How To Find Your Judging Session Information

Before the event, log in to the Jamboree website to see your Judging Session information. This will include the link to your Zoom meeting, the local time and the room number of your Judging Session.

How To Join Your Judging Session: In-person Teams And Participants
How To: The Judging Session
What to bring:
A 5-minute update or overview of your project. See the Recommendations below for more guidance.
A laptop to connect to Zoom and show the 5-minute presentation. The presentation/Zoom laptop must be able to connect to the internet via an ethernet or USB-C cable. WiFi will not be available to stream presentations. Please make sure your laptop battery is fully charged.
How to participate

READY! 10 minutes before your start time:

Gather in the Judging Session waiting area in front of the Judging Rooms.
On the laptop, join the Zoom Waiting Room by signing in to jamboree.igem.org and opening the Zoom link.
Note for hybrid teams: Any remote team members should also enter the Zoom Waiting Room at this time.

SET! 5 minutes before your start time:

You will be automatically admitted to the Zoom meeting to connect you to remote judges and/or teammates.
Enter the physical Judging Session room and connect your laptop to the ethernet or USB-C connector.

GO! At the start time, the Judging Session will begin!

Team update or overview (5 minutes): Present a short overview or a project update. If you wish to show slides, a video, Wiki pages or other materials on your laptop, you must present them using the Share Screen feature in Zoom. See the recommendations section below for more guidance about materials for your Team Update or Overview.
Question and answer time (20 minutes): Your judges will use this time to ask your team questions. To help respond to questions, you can refer to your Team Wiki or backup slides by using the Share Screen feature in Zoom.
Only team student members and student team leaders listed on the official Team Roster can present the 5-minute update or overview and answer questions during the Q&A session. Advisors, instructors and PIs are not permitted to present or answer questions about the project. Team PIs, advisors and instructors are welcome to sit in the back of the room or log in to the Zoom meeting, but they can only stay as silent audience members.

FINISH! At the finish time, the Judging Session will end.

Exit the physical judging room AND Zoom meeting on time. The next team will enter and begin setting up as you leave.
How To Join Your Judging Session: Remote Teams and Participants
Prepare before the Judging Session:
A 5-minute update or overview of your project. See the Recommendations below for more guidance.
How to participate:

READY! 10 minutes before your start time:

Join the Zoom Waiting Room by signing in to jamboree.igem.org and opening the Zoom link.

SET! 5 minutes before your start time:

You will be admitted from the Zoom Waiting Room to the Zoom meeting.
Test your audio, video and share screen functions in Zoom at this time.

GO! At the start time, the Judging Session will begin!

Team update or overview (5 minutes): Present a short overview or a project update. You will be able to share your screen to show slides, a video, Wiki pages or other materials on your computer using the Share Screen feature in Zoom. See the recommendations section below for more guidance about materials for your Team update or overview.
Question and answer time (20 minutes): Your judges will use this time to ask your team questions. To help respond to questions, you can refer to your Team Wiki or backup slides by using the Share Screen feature in Zoom.
Only team student members and student team leaders listed on the official Team Roster can present the 5-minute update or overview and answer questions during the Q&A session. Advisors, instructors and PIs are not permitted to present or answer questions about the project. Team PIs, advisors and instructors are welcome to log in to the Zoom meeting, but they can only stay as silent audience members.

FINISH! At the finish time, the Judging Session will end.

Exit the Zoom meeting on time.
Recommendations
Tips For The 5 Minute Overview/Update
Do not try to repeat your whole presentation, project or Wiki. Judges already have access to your Wiki and presentation video, and you should assume that they have already reviewed your whole project before the Judging Session. The 5-minute overview/update is your opportunity to update the judges on any progress you have made since the Wiki Freeze, or to remind the judges about the strongest parts of your project.
We recommend you prepare 4 - 6 slides. A sample slideshow could include:
1 overview slide: About the problem.
1 methods slide: How you attacked the problem.
1 - 2 results slides: Show data behind key findings and results.
1 “next steps” slide: What would you do next, if you were given more time?
If desired, you could add backup slides with more data and information to anticipate judges’ questions, but do not present them.
Tips For The Q&A Portion
Before the Jamboree, prepare for predictable questions:
Ask your team PIs and instructors to go through a mock Judging Session and have them ask questions they think will come up.
If possible, ask other members of your department or school to also review your project and ask you questions. It can be very helpful to get outside perspectives!
Prepare a few backup slides that can address the predictable questions easily.
During the Judging Session, you can share your screen at any time to help answer a question. You can bring up your Wiki pages or any prepared slides at any time.
What should you do if you still don’t know the answer to a question? Admit it! There is no harm in acknowledging gaps in your knowledge; good scientists recognize gaps as more opportunities for future research.
Remember, it's the judges' job to ask you detailed questions about your work - they have watched your presentation video, they have been reading your Wiki and are well versed in your project already. They are excited to talk to you! The judges may probe the edges of your knowledge, but they also want you to succeed!
Judging Sessions must use a common language. At the Grand Jamboree the common language is English. However, iGEM is a global organization and most iGEM participants first learned other languages. If judges or students don’t understand each other, it’s okay to ask for the question or the answer to be repeated or stated in another way. You can also ask that the question or the answer be typed in the Zoom chat feature. Let’s work together to communicate the best science possible!

name: deliverable_attributions
title: Project Attributions Form | iGEM Competition
content:
PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

Project Attributions Form

In the iGEM Competition, we celebrate student effort and achievement. The Project Attributions Form helps the judges differentiate between what students accomplished from the ways their external collaborators supported them. Therefore, teams must clearly explain on the Project Attributions Form what work they conducted by themselves and what work was done by others.

All students in the iGEM Competition rely on outside help. As examples, students reference previous supporting work by other scientists or previous iGEM teams; they receive guidance and advice from advisors and experts; they receive materials, such as parts, plasmids or cell lines from other labs; or they may contract specific tasks with professionals where their own facilities or skills fall short. These and other supporting activities are normal parts of the scientific process. Outside help is allowed in the iGEM Competition as long as the activities performed, and who performed them, are reported with honesty and clarity.

The Project Attributions Form deliverable was created to promote the iGEM value of “Intellectual Integrity”, and it helps us to recognize and celebrate what our incredible students can do.

Deadline
OCTOBER
8
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Project Attributions Form Freeze

Complete and submit your Project Attributions Form.

Requirements

Teams must use the standard Project Attributions Form. Do not create your own form or format. Any attributions information on the Wiki that is not a part of the standard form will not be accepted or judged.

1. Standard URL

Your Project Attributions Form should be documented on the Team Wiki on the Project Attributions page using the Standard URL: https://2025.igem.wiki/team-name/attributions, with "team-name" being your registered team name (ex: Team Example's URL would be: https://2025.igem.wiki/example/attributions). Learn more about Standard URL Pages on the Team Wiki deliverable requirements.

2. Attributions Form iframe

The following iframe code is required to display the data from your Project Attributions Form on the Team Wiki attributions page. DO NOT DELETE this code from the attributions.html file on your team's GitLab repository.

<script type="text/javascript">
  // iframe height listener script
</script>
<iframe
  style="width: 100%"
  id="igem-attribution-form"
  src="https://teams.igem.org/wiki/{teamID}/attributions"
>
</iframe>

If you use another framework to build your Wiki, for instance React, make sure you are still embedding the original <iframe src="https://teams.igem.org/wiki/{teamID}/attributions"></iframe> element, where {teamID} is replaced by the numerical ID of the team (5032 for team Example, for instance).

3. Content
Each team must clearly attribute the work completed by the student team members, instructors, advisors and PIs on the team roster, using the standard Project Attributions Form.
The team must distinguish work completed by the team members from work completed by any individual not on the current year's Team Roster. This includes, but is not limited to, acknowledging the work done by other iGEM teams, including previous teams from your school or institution; contractors or technicians; students in the PI’s laboratory but not on the Team Roster; and collaborators.
See detailed instructions below.

Note: This requirement is not about literature references - literature references should be displayed throughout your entire Wiki!

Recommendations

Here are a few tips for succeeding on the Attributions Form:

Start early. As soon as you can access the form, start filling it out. You can make as many changes as you need to until the form is frozen on the due date.
Fill out all three tables in the Project Attributions Form completely, accurately and honestly. Missing or erroneous information may affect a team’s eligibility for Competition awards and/or personal certificates. Disciplinary action based on incomplete, inaccurate or dishonest Project Attributions Forms can occur at any time during the competition cycle including before, during and after the Jamboree.
Too much information is better than too little. Wondering if you should add something to this form? Do it. There is no length limit to this form.
Examples of Attributions Pages

As you fill out your Attributions Form (see the instructions below), the information will automatically populate on the Project Attributions page on your Team Wiki. The way the information is displayed on your Wiki will look different from how it’s displayed in the original form.

Here are some examples of Attributions Forms displayed on the Wikis of some of the Grand Prize winners:

2023 Grand Prize High School Winner Japan United’s Attributions Page. ↗
2023 Grand Prize Undergraduate Winner McGill’s Attributions Page. ↗
2024 Grand Prize Overgraduate Winner Marburg’s Attributions Page. ↗
Frequently Asked Questions

Can I base my project on a previous one?

How do we report work done by former members of the team?

Is the Attributions Form the only place I can list sponsors and/or other supporters?

What if I don’t use the green "Submit" button before the freeze?

I have a better way of showing the content on my Wiki - can I use my own format?

Is there a limit to how many people or entities I can name or add to this form?

How To Edit The Attributions Form
Deliverables Dashboard

Edit your Attributions Form through your team's Deliverables Dashboard.

Navigate to the team list and select your team.
Scroll down to the “Team Deliverables Overview” section and click the “Deliverables Dashboard” button.
Locate "Project Attributions Form" in the table and click the green arrow to proceed.
Specific instructions for each component of the Attributions Form can be found below, on this page.

Team List

The standard Attributions Form consists of three tables:

Table 1: Team Member Contributions - Lists what each team member accomplished.
Table 2: External Contributions - Lists what external parties (i.e., individuals, groups or institutions not on the current iGEM Team Roster) accomplished.
Table 3: Project Timeline - Lists the start and end dates of different parts of the project.

Specific instructions and examples for each table follow:

Table 1: Team Member Contributions

Table 1 answers the question, “What did each team member contribute to the project?” In Table 1, you must clearly attribute the work completed by the student team members, instructors, advisors and PIs on your team roster.

Example Table:

Instructions: How To Edit Team Member Contributions

Table 2: External Contributions

Table 2 answers the question, "What did each external partner contribute to the project?" You must distinguish work completed by the team members from work completed by any individual not on the current year's team roster. This includes, but is not limited to, acknowledging the work done by other iGEM teams, including previous teams from your school or institution; contractors or technicians; students in the PI’s laboratory but not on the iGEM Team Roster; and collaborators.

Example Table:

Instructions: How To Edit External Contributions

Table 3 : Project Timeline

Table 3 outlines when different parts of the project work was initiated and completed.

Example Table:

Instructions: How To Edit The Project Timeline

name: deliverable_presentation_video
title: Presentation Video | iGEM Competition
content:
PINNED
COMING SOON...

Preparations for the 2026 iGEM Competition are underway. This page will be updated soon. Below are details from the 2025 Competition season for reference.

Presentation Video

The presentation video is your opportunity to communicate the details of your project to a broad audience. It should be clear, engaging and informative, focusing on the technical content and scientific details of your project. Your video should succinctly cover the project design, results, data analysis, and conclusions, while also highlighting your Human Practices work and other team activities.

Questions? Ask on the #competition-video channel of the iGEM 2025 Global Slack.

Deadline
OCTOBER
15
15:00 (GMT)
DEADLINE
Presentation Video Due

Upload your Presentation Video by this deadline. Please avoid submitting in the minutes just before this deadline, in case there are technical issues.

Requirements
Technical Specifications
File Size: Maximum file size of 1 GB.
Duration: The video must not exceed 15 minutes (900 seconds). Do not speed up a longer video to fit the time limit.
Format: Submit your video in .MP4 format.
Resolution: Full HD (1920 x 1080) or higher (4K: 3840 × 2160).
Aspect ratio: 16:9, landscape orientation.
Content Requirements
Credits: Attribute all contributors at the end of the video with on-screen text for at least 2 seconds. Include external collaborators if applicable.
Copyrighted material is NOT allowed: Use only appropriately licensed materials (e.g., Creative Commons media). All music must be royalty free or licensed for Creative Commons use, with proper attribution.
Consent: Obtain informed consent from anyone appearing in your video. Parental consent is required for minors under 18. Review the Safety Policies on interviews.
Rules of Conduct: Ensure your video adheres to iGEM's values and Rules of Conduct. Harassment, offensive material, disturbing images, violence or the display of country flags are strictly prohibited.
Captions, Narration, and Text
Captions: English captions will be automatically generated, but they might be inaccurate. Once available, you may download, review, correct, and re-upload them as a separate .vtt ↗ file. Do not embed captions in the video itself. Additional captions in other languages are encouraged.
Narration Language: The primary narration must be in English.
Multilingual Narration: Consider recording additional versions in your native language or other languages your team speaks. This can benefit you in reaching out to your local communities. Multiple videos with different audio tracks can be uploaded, though this is optional.

Please note: An iGEM introduction sequence will automatically be added to the beginning of your video, once your video has been reviewed by our moderators.

Recommendations

Consider including the following elements in your presentation video:

Introduction
Project background
Project goals
Methodology overview
Results, including data and key findings
Human Practices used
Safety considerations
Next steps
Attributions
The video should be a creative effort by your team

While you can collaborate with external parties or outsource some work, the ideas and overall effort should primarily come from your team.

Audio is important!

Pay attention to audio recording quality. Visit our Videos Hub for tips on planning, filming, editing and equipment recommendations: Video Production 101: 2021.igem.org/Videos/Production

Examples
How To Submit Your Video

Deliverables Dashboard

Submit your Presentation Video through your team’s Deliverables Dashboard.

Note: The primary narration for your video must be in English. You will be prompted to upload the English version of your video first. You are welcome to upload additional versions of your videos in other languages as well, though doing so is optional!

Navigate to the team list and select your team.
Scroll down to the "Team Deliverables Overview" section and click the "Deliverables Dashboard" button.
Click on "Presentation Video" in the table.
Click on "Proceed to Upload" and follow the prompts to upload your file.
While the video is uploading, complete the required fields: "Title", "Description" and "Language". Note: The first version uploaded must be narrated in English.
You may optionally provide a "thumbnail" image or "caption" files in other languages.
To upload additional narration language versions, repeat steps 4-6.
Once the captions have been automatically generated, you may download, review and re-upload them.

Team List

name: faq
title: FAQ | iGEM Competition
content:
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

Below we have some of the most frequently asked questions for the iGEM competition. If your question is not answered below, please email us at hq AT igem DOT org and we'll help you!

Human Practices
On Integrated Human Practices
1. What does it mean for Human Practices to be "integrated"?

Your team’s Human Practices work is “integrated” when you go beyond just considering whether your project is responsible and good for the world, but also demonstrate how you respond to your Human Practices reflections, research, and/or engagement. Use the design/build/test/learn cycle of engineering as a model and show us how social feedback or investigation has informed your process.

There are many ways you could respond to your Human Practices, such as:

planning a different application than what you’d originally intended (e.g. Calgary 2019 realizing that synthetic biology could impact every stage of canola production, not just oil processing after stakeholder interviews)
updating how end users would interact with your project (e.g. TU Delft 2017 ensuring their diagnostic product could be interpreted with the naked eye)
using alternative methods (e.g. deciding to use gene editing techniques rather than recombinant DNA after learning about local regulations on GMOs)
choosing a new way to communicate your results (e.g. Marburg 2018 finding ways to express their results for visually-impaired listeners)
proposing a regulation or other framework to improve your project’s impact (e.g. suggesting changes to biosecurity DNA screening based on what you learn about non-canonical amino acids)

Any of these responses could be considered “integrating” Human Practices into your project. You can find many more examples on the Teams website. If you’re wondering about the difference between the Human Practices criteria for the Gold and Silver medals, you’ll find related guidance on the Medals page.

2. How important is Integrated Human Practices compared to regular Human Practices?

Integrated Human Practices is just taking your Human Practices work to its logical conclusion, and completing a loop between feedback and project design. When you investigate Human Practices issues related to your project, you should also be asking: what does this mean for how you’re implementing your project? Were you right about who would benefit from your project? Did you discover any concerns about your approach? If you take those questions seriously, and act upon the answers you find, then you will be doing Integrated Human Practices.

3. Do we have to find a stakeholder to tell us what’s wrong with our project?

No. Changing your project based on stakeholder feedback is one way to show you took your Human Practices work seriously, but there are many kinds of Human Practices work that your team could respond to and integrate into your project. In addition to stakeholder engagement, the Exemplary Projects page includes examples of market assessment, policy analysis, ethical explorations, and many other forms of Integrated Human Practices.

Types of work that count as Human Practices
1. Does outreach or educational work count as Human Practices?

Not usually. There are many ways for teams to engage with the public that do not count towards the Human Practices medal requirements, and one of the most common mistakes made by iGEM teams is to classify their public outreach or education activities as Human Practices.

This mistake occurs because outreach activities involve exchanges between your team and the public. However, they do not usually lead to an interchange of ideas between your project and society. To be integrated, your Human Practices work must impact your project’s purpose, design and/or execution.

Although they do not always qualify as Human Practices, education and outreach are important contributions to science in general. To that end, there are special prizes, like the Education prize or the Inclusivity Award, through which your team’s outreach work might be rewarded. These prizes recognize excellent work to establish dialogue with new communities and to engage and involve new people in synthetic biology. You can learn more about these other prizes on the Awards page.

2. My team got advice from a professor at our university about our project– does this count as Human Practices?

We encourage teams to draw from as much expertise as possible in developing their projects. However, part of the goal of Human Practices at iGEM is to encourage teams to engage with issues that extend beyond the lab and even beyond their institution. While seeking expertise from professors may make up a part of your team’s Human Practices effort, we strongly recommend you also seek out input from more diverse communities.

3. If we get feedback on our public engagement work, will that count as Integrated Human Practices?

If the feedback from public engagement affects the purpose, design and/or execution of your project, then it counts as Integrated Human Practices. As one example, the public response to an art exhibit run by your team might raise new ethical or societal issues, causing you to refocus your project. As another example, user feedback on an educational hardware or software tool could cause your team to develop a new way to report your synthetic biology work.

If the feedback does not impact the design or execution of your main project idea, it probably won't count as Integrated Human Practices. However, gathering feedback is still important! It will let you develop better public engagement, will allow you to provide good documentation of your Human Practices methods, and could help your team win special prizes such as Best Education, Best Software Tool, or Best Presentation. You can learn more about these other prizes on the Awards page.

4. My team has an idea for something creative. How do we know if it counts as Human Practices?

Exploring and testing new and creative ideas is something we strongly encourage. We suggest that you take a look at past projects that may have tried similar ideas. If your creative work ties into the design and development of other aspects of your project, it may be considered Integrated Human Practices. If you’re not sure about how to integrate your creative ideas into your project, feel free to contact the Human Practices committee for guidance.

How to do Human Practices
1. Do we need institutional research ethics approval for our Human Practices work?

That depends on your institution. Only your university’s research ethics board (or equivalent research ethics body) has the authority to decide what counts as human subjects research and what requires a human subjects protocol.

Low-risk activities like some direct stakeholder engagements may be exempt from institutional review. Many institutions don’t count informal conversations as “research” unless they are part of a systematic study (such as a survey) or are intended to create publishable, generalized knowledge.

We expect your team to determine which rules apply, to comply with local regulations on research involving human subjects, and to follow valid scientific survey design. You should review the iGEM policy on Human Subjects Research and the sections of the Resources page on informed consent and writing valid surveys.

2. How can my team connect with people or communities related to our project? Where do we start?

You don’t necessarily need to start by connecting with individual people. You can do a background literature review on the societal aspects of your project, just as you will have done for its scientific aspects. Which research teams, especially in the social sciences, have investigated the problem your team is working on?

If you do want to speak with individuals, you might want to start by contacting local businesses or nonprofits that do work related to your project or with experts whose work you’ve come across in your research. Many people will be happy to speak directly to passionate iGEM team members.

When contacting someone, describe who you are and why you’re reaching out to them. Leave short emails or messages with specific questions, so it’s easy for them to respond if they have a tight schedule. Even if they don’t have time to talk, you can ask them to recommend other people for you to contact.

3. Is my team allowed to work on a controversial project? What kind of Human Practices should we do?

A project might be called controversial for many reasons— maybe you think you’re likely to see sensationalist media coverage of your work, maybe your project is connected to hotly-debated ethical issues. These may be the sort of issues that iGEM teams investigate through their Human Practices work. Whether or not your project idea is controversial doesn’t say much about its value.

However, your team will not be rewarded for seeking out controversy, and you should not pursue an idea simply because it’s controversial. Ask yourself whether you’ve found the best approach: are there other ways to solve the same problems? Teams are expected to document the rationale and process by which they select their project. A decision not to use certain methods or pursue certain ideas is a perfectly valid outcome of your Human Practices work, which you can document on your wiki.

If your team decides to go ahead with a controversial project, ensure you treat high-stakes issues with respect. Remember that iGEM students are ambassadors for the global synthetic biology community; what your team does this year may have a large impact beyond the competition. We also recognize that iGEM is an international community, and issues that are contentious and polarizing in one country or culture may already be settled in another.

4. How can my team integrate Human Practices into our Foundational Advance project?

Even projects which aim to develop the technical aspects of synthetic biology, rather than apply them to a real-world problem, can have important societal implications. Some examples of Integrated Human Practices in Foundational Advance projects include Marburg 2018, who integrated what they learned from their engagement with visually-impaired high school students to make both their wiki and presentation accessible, and Heidelberg 2017, who developed software to screen DNA sequences produced by their directed evolution algorithms for any potential safety concerns. Your team could also look into issues of intellectual property, or do stakeholder consultations, with members of the scientific community as stakeholders.

5. What if we want to patent our work?

One of iGEM’s core values is cooperation, which is expressed through the "Get, Give, & Share" philosophy of the Registry of Standard Biological Parts, the open documentation of team wikis, and many other aspects of the competition.

That said, we recognize that your team may wish to secure intellectual property protection for your work, perhaps because, like many iGEM teams before you, you intend to start a company based on your work. Intellectual property law varies greatly between countries, and we encourage you to contact your institution for support in navigating it. Many universities have a Technology Transfer Office- if you have access to one, that's likely a good place to start. You may find the resources offered by the World Intellectual Property Organization, such as country profiles, helpful.

Some previous iGEM teams have documented their thinking about intellectual property. You can read about the TUDelft 2017 and Stockholm 2018 teams applying for EU patents, and the Manchester 2017 team’s write-up on intellectual property and synthetic biology, which also covers their decision to decide not to pursue a patent.
